revision.comment: [[WP:AES|←]]新しいページ: ''''八乙女'''（やおとめ）とは、主に[[神楽]]や[[舞]]（いわゆる[[巫女神楽]]・[[巫女舞]]）をもって奉仕する8人の[[巫女]]の...'
revision.text:
'''八乙女'''（やおとめ）とは、主に[[神楽]]や[[舞]]（いわゆる[[巫女神楽]]・[[巫女舞]]）をもって奉仕する8人の[[巫女]]のこと。'''八社女'''・'''八少女'''・'''八乎止女'''ともいう。また、略して'''八女'''（やめ）とも。
古くは[[景行天皇]]の[[大嘗祭]]の際に[[天皇]]と[[神々]]に食事を奉仕した巫女に由来するとも言われており、後には[[神祇官]]において卜定められた[[采女]]がこうした任務にあたり、後には他の神事にも用いられるようになったとされている。ただし、人数が8人と定まったのは後世の事であり、古くは「八」の字は複数あるいは多くという意味で使われていたものであるとされている。
古くは[[春日大社]]や[[厳島神社]]の八乙女がよく知られていたが、今日では[[美保神社]]や[[金刀比羅宮]]、[[豊国神社]]の八乙女舞も有名である。また、[[明治時代]]に政府の方針で巫女舞そのものが危機に瀕した際に春日大社の八乙女舞を元にして、巫女舞そのものがより芸術性の高い舞へと変革されていったともいわれている。

revision.comment: カテゴリー・スタブ項目無しだと寂しい・・・
revision.text:
'''八乙女'''（やおとめ）とは、主に[[神楽]]や[[舞]]（いわゆる[[巫女神楽]]・[[巫女舞]]）をもって奉仕する8人の[[巫女]]のこと。'''八社女'''・'''八少女'''・'''八乎止女'''ともいう。また、略して'''八女'''（やめ）とも。
古くは[[景行天皇]]の[[大嘗祭]]の際に[[天皇]]と[[神々]]に食事を奉仕した巫女に由来するとも言われており、後には[[神祇官]]において卜定められた[[采女]]がこうした任務にあたり、後には他の神事にも用いられるようになったとされている。ただし、人数が8人と定まったのは後世の事であり、古くは「八」の字は複数あるいは多くという意味で使われていたものであるとされている。
古くは[[春日大社]]や[[厳島神社]]の八乙女がよく知られていたが、今日では[[美保神社]]や[[金刀比羅宮]]、[[豊国神社]]の八乙女舞も有名である。また、[[明治時代]]に政府の方針で巫女舞そのものが危機に瀕した際に春日大社の八乙女舞を元にして、巫女舞そのものがより芸術性の高い舞へと変革されていったともいわれている。
==備考== [[宮城県]][[仙台市]][[泉区]]に[[八乙女 (仙台市)|八乙女]]という地名がある。
{{shinto-stub}} [[category:神道|やおとめ]]

'''八乙女'''（やおとめ）とは、主に[[神楽]]や[[舞]]（いわゆる[[巫女神楽]]・[[巫女舞]]）をもって奉仕する8人の[[巫女]]のこと。'''八社女'''・'''八少女'''・'''八乎止女'''ともいう。また、略して'''八女'''（やめ）とも。
古くは[[景行天皇]]の[[大嘗祭]]の際に[[天皇]]と[[神々]]に食事を奉仕した巫女に由来するとされている。後には[[神祇官]]において卜定められた[[采女]]がこうした任務にあたり、後には他の[[神社]]においても同様の役目の巫女が置かれ、更には神事にも関わるようになったと考えられている。ただし、人数が8人と定まったのは後世の事であり、古くは「八」の字は複数あるいは多くという意味で使われていたものであると考えられている。
古くは[[春日大社]]や[[厳島神社]]の八乙女がよく知られていたが、今日では[[美保神社]]や[[金刀比羅宮]]、[[豊国神社]]の八乙女舞も有名である。また、[[明治時代]]に政府の方針で巫女舞そのものが危機に瀕した際に春日大社の八乙女舞を元にして、巫女舞そのものがより芸術性の高い舞へと変革されていったともいわれている。
==備考== [[宮城県]][[仙台市]][[泉区]]に[[八乙女 (仙台市)|八乙女]]という地名がある。
{{shinto-stub}} [[category:神道|やおとめ]]

'''八乙女'''（やおとめ）とは、主に[[神楽]]や[[舞]]（いわゆる[[巫女神楽]]・[[巫女舞]]）をもって奉仕する8人の[[巫女]]のこと。'''八社女'''・'''八少女'''・'''八乎止女'''ともいう。また、略して'''八女'''（やめ）とも。

ただし、人数が8人と定まったのは後世の事であり、古くは「八」の字は複数あるいは多くという意味で使われていたものであると考えられている。 [[古代]]の[[景行天皇]]の[[大嘗祭]]の際に[[天皇]]と[[神々]]に食事を奉仕した巫女に由来するとされている。後には[[神祇官]]において卜定められた[[采女]]がこうした任務にあたった。この影響を受けて他の[[神社]]においても同様の役目の巫女が置かれ、更には神事にも関わるようになったとされている。 古くは[[春日大社]]や[[厳島神社]]の八乙女がよく知られていたが、今日では[[美保神社]]や[[金刀比羅宮]]、[[豊国神社]]の八乙女舞も有名である。また、[[明治時代]]に政府の方針で巫女舞そのものが危機に瀕した際に春日大社の八乙女舞を元にして、巫女舞そのものがより芸術性の高い舞へと変革されていったともいわれている。 ==備考== [[宮城県]][[仙台市]][[泉区 (仙台市)|泉区]]に[[八乙女 (仙台市)|八乙女]]という地名がある。 [[富山県]][[南砺市]]に八乙女山という山がある。平成の大合併で新市の名称を八乙女市にする案もあったが市議会で多数決により南砺市に決定した。また京都の十文字焼きに似た八文字焼きという行事が毎年行われている。 {{shinto-stub}} [[category:神道|やおとめ]]

ただし、人数が8人と定まったのは後世の事であり、古くは「八」の字は複数あるいは多くという意味で使われていたものであると考えられている。 [[古代]]の[[景行天皇]]の[[大嘗祭]]の際に[[天皇]]と[[神々]]に食事を奉仕した巫女に由来するとされている。後には[[神祇官]]において卜定められた[[采女]]がこうした任務にあたった。この影響を受けて他の[[神社]]においても同様の役目の巫女が置かれ、更には神事にも関わるようになったとされている。 古くは[[春日大社]]や[[厳島神社]]の八乙女がよく知られていたが、今日では[[美保神社]]や[[金刀比羅宮]]、[[豊国神社]]の八乙女舞も有名である。また、[[明治時代]]に政府の方針で巫女舞そのものが危機に瀕した際に春日大社の八乙女舞を元にして、巫女舞そのものがより芸術性の高い舞へと変革されていったともいわれている。 ==備考== [[秋田県]][[大仙市]]に八乙女公園がある。八乙女城に由来。 [[宮城県]][[仙台市]][[泉区 (仙台市)|泉区]]に[[八乙女 (仙台市)|八乙女]]という地名がある。 [[富山県]][[南砺市]]に八乙女山という山がある。平成の大合併で新市の名称を八乙女市にする案もあったが市議会で多数決により南砺市に決定した。また京都の[[大文字焼き]]に似た八文字焼きという行事が毎年行われている。 {{shinto-stub}} [[category:神道|やおとめ]]

ただし、人数が8人と定まったのは後世の事であり、古くは「八」の字は複数あるいは多くという意味で使われていたものであると考えられている。 [[古代]]の[[景行天皇]]の[[大嘗祭]]の際に[[天皇]]と[[神々]]に食事を奉仕した巫女に由来するとされている。後には[[神祇官]]において卜定められた[[采女]]がこうした任務にあたった。この影響を受けて他の[[神社]]においても同様の役目の巫女が置かれ、更には神事にも関わるようになったとされている。 古くは[[春日大社]]や[[厳島神社]]の八乙女がよく知られていたが、今日では[[美保神社]]や[[金刀比羅宮]]、[[豊国神社]]の八乙女舞も有名である。また、[[明治時代]]に政府の方針で巫女舞そのものが危機に瀕した際に春日大社の八乙女舞を元にして、巫女舞そのものがより芸術性の高い舞へと変革されていったともいわれている。 ==備考== [[秋田県]][[大仙市]]に八乙女公園がある。八乙女城に由来。 [[宮城県]][[仙台市]][[泉区 (仙台市)|泉区]]に[[八乙女 (仙台市)|八乙女]]という地名がある。 [[富山県]][[南砺市]]に八乙女山という山がある。平成の大合併で新市の名称を八乙女市にする案もあったが市議会で多数決により南砺市に決定した。また京都の[[大文字焼き]]に似た八文字焼きという行事が毎年行われている。 ==関連項目== *[[巫女]] {{DEFAULTSORT:やおとめ}} {{shinto-stub}} [[category:神道]]

'''八乙女'''（やおとめ）とは、主に[[神楽]]や[[舞]]（いわゆる[[巫女神楽]]・[[巫女舞]]）をもって奉仕する8人の[[巫女]]のこと。'''八社女'''・'''八少女'''・'''八乎止女'''とも書く。また、略して'''八女'''（やめ）とも。
ただし、人数が8人と定まったのは後世の事であり、古くは「八」の字は複数あるいは多くという意味で使われていたものであると考えられている。
[[古代]]の[[景行天皇]]の[[大嘗祭]]の際に[[天皇]]と[[神々]]に食事を奉仕した巫女に由来するとされている。後には[[神祇官]]において卜定められた[[采女]]がこうした任務にあたった。この影響を受けて他の[[神社]]においても同様の役目の巫女が置かれ、更には神事にも関わるようになったとされている。
古くは[[春日大社]]や[[厳島神社]]の八乙女がよく知られていたが、今日では[[美保神社]]や[[金刀比羅宮]]、[[豊国神社]]の八乙女舞も有名である。また、[[明治時代]]に政府の方針で巫女舞そのものが危機に瀕した際に春日大社の八乙女舞を元にして、巫女舞そのものがより芸術性の高い舞へと変革されていったともいわれている。
==地名== [[秋田県]][[大仙市]]に八乙女公園がある。八乙女城に由来。
[[宮城県]][[仙台市]][[泉区 (仙台市)|泉区]]に[[八乙女 (仙台市)|八乙女]]という地名がある。
[[富山県]][[南砺市]]に八乙女山という山がある。平成の大合併で新市の名称を八乙女市にする案もあったが市議会で多数決により南砺市に決定した。また京都の[[大文字焼き]]に似た八文字焼きという行事が毎年行われている。

revision.comment: 参照: 倉林正次「八乙女」（『神道辞典』（堀書店、1968年））
revision.text:
'''八乙女'''（やおとめ）とは、主に[[神楽]]や[[舞]]（いわゆる[[巫女神楽]]・[[巫女舞]]）をもって奉仕する8人の[[巫女]]のこと。'''八社女'''・'''八少女'''・'''八乎止女'''とも書く。また、略して'''八女'''（やめ）とも。
==概要== ただし、人数が8人と定まったのは後世の事であり、古くは「八」の字は複数あるいは多くという意味で使われていたもので、神霊を扱う神聖な処女の意味があったと言われている。また、巫女の群遊の場合には「七」という数字が用いられる事例がある（『[[古事記]]』[[高佐士野]]の説話など）。
[[古代]]の[[景行天皇]]の[[大嘗祭]]の際に[[天皇]]と[[神々]]に食事を奉仕した巫女に由来するとされている。後には[[神祇官]]において卜定められた[[采女]]がこうした任務にあたった。この影響を受けて他の[[神社]]においても同様の役目の巫女が置かれ、更には神事にも関わるようになったとされている。
八乙女による巫女神楽や巫女舞を「八乙女神楽」・「八乙女舞」と呼ぶ。古くは[[春日大社]]や[[厳島神社]]の八乙女がよく知られていたが、今日では[[美保神社]]や[[金刀比羅宮]]、[[豊国神社 (京都市)|豊国神社]]の八乙女舞も有名になっている。また、[[明治時代]]に政府の方針で巫女舞そのものが危機に瀕した際には、春日大社の八乙女舞を元にして巫女舞そのものがより芸術性の高い舞へと変革されていったともいわれている。
==地名== *八乙女浦（[[山形県]][[鶴岡市]]由良）：[[蜂子皇子]]を、舞を踊って出迎えた八人の美しい乙女に由来する。 *八乙女洞窟（山形県鶴岡市由良）：明治時代の酒田地震により、崩れた岩で入口が塞がっている。 *八乙女公園（[[秋田県]][[大仙市]]）：八乙女城に由来する。 *[[八乙女 (仙台市)|八乙女]]（[[宮城県]][[仙台市]][[泉区 (仙台市)|泉区]]） *八乙女山（[[富山県]][[南砺市]]）：平成の大合併で新市名を八乙女市にする案もあったが、市議会の多数決により南砺市に決定した。また京都の[[五山送り火|大文字焼き]]に似た八文字焼きという行事が毎年行われている。
==関連項目== *[[巫女]]
{{DEFAULTSORT:やおとめ}} [[category:神道]] [[category:聖職者]] {{shinto-stub}}

'''八乙女'''（やおとめ）とは、主に[[神楽]]や[[舞]]（いわゆる[[巫女神楽]]・[[巫女舞]]）をもって奉仕する8人の[[巫女]]のこと。'''八社女'''・'''八少女'''・'''八乎止女'''とも書く。また、略して'''八女'''（やめ）とも。
==概要== ただし、人数が8人と定まったのは後世の事であり、古くは「八」の字は複数あるいは多くという意味で使われていたもので、神霊を扱う神聖な処女の意味があったと言われている。また、巫女の群遊の場合には「七」という数字が用いられる事例がある（『[[古事記]]』[[高佐士野]]の説話など）。
[[古代]]の[[景行天皇]]の[[大嘗祭]]の際に[[天皇]]と[[神々]]に食事を奉仕した巫女に由来するとされている。後には[[神祇官]]において卜定められた[[采女]]がこうした任務にあたった。この影響を受けて他の[[神社]]においても同様の役目の巫女が置かれ、更には神事にも関わるようになったとされている。
八乙女による巫女神楽や巫女舞を「八乙女神楽」・「八乙女舞」と呼ぶ。古くは[[春日大社]]や[[厳島神社]]の八乙女がよく知られていたが、今日では[[美保神社]]や[[金刀比羅宮]]、[[豊国神社 (京都市)|豊国神社]]の八乙女舞も有名になっている。また、[[明治時代]]に政府の方針で巫女舞そのものが危機に瀕した際には、春日大社の八乙女舞を元にして巫女舞そのものがより芸術性の高い舞へと変革されていったともいわれている。
==地名== *八乙女浦（[[山形県]][[鶴岡市]]由良）：[[蜂子皇子]]を、舞を踊って出迎えた八人の美しい乙女に由来する。 *八乙女洞窟（山形県鶴岡市由良）：明治時代の酒田地震により、崩れた岩で入口が塞がっている。 *八乙女公園（[[秋田県]][[大仙市]]）：八乙女城に由来する。 *[[八乙女 (仙台市)|八乙女]]（[[宮城県]][[仙台市]][[泉区 (仙台市)|泉区]]） *八乙女山（[[富山県]][[南砺市]]）：平成の大合併で新市名を八乙女市にする案もあったが、市議会の多数決により南砺市に決定した。また京都の[[五山送り火|大文字焼き]]に似た八文字焼きという行事が毎年行われている。
==関連項目== *[[巫女]]
{{DEFAULTSORT:やおとめ}} [[category:神道]] [[category:聖職者]] [[Category:神楽]] {{shinto-stub}}

revision.text:
'''八乙女'''（やおとめ）とは、主に[[神楽]]や[[舞]]（いわゆる[[巫女神楽]]・[[巫女舞]]）をもって奉仕する8人の[[巫女]]のこと。'''八社女'''・'''八少女'''・'''八乎止女'''とも書く。また、略して'''八女'''（やめ）とも。
==概要== ただし、人数が8人と定まったのは後世の事であり、古くは「八」の字は複数あるいは多くという意味で使われていたもので、神霊を扱う神聖な処女の意味があったと言われている。また、巫女の群遊の場合には「七」という数字が用いられる事例がある（『[[古事記]]』[[高佐士野]]の説話など）。
[[古代]]の[[景行天皇]]の[[大嘗祭]]の際に[[天皇]]と[[神々]]に食事を奉仕した巫女に由来するとされている。後には[[神祇官]]において卜定められた[[采女]]がこうした任務にあたった。この影響を受けて他の[[神社]]においても同様の役目の巫女が置かれ、更には神事にも関わるようになったとされている。
八乙女による巫女神楽や巫女舞を「八乙女神楽」・「八乙女舞」と呼ぶ。古くは[[春日大社]]や[[厳島神社]]の八乙女がよく知られていたが、今日では[[美保神社]]や[[金刀比羅宮]]、[[豊国神社 (京都市)|豊国神社]]の八乙女舞も有名になっている。また、[[明治時代]]に政府の方針で巫女舞そのものが危機に瀕した際には、春日大社の八乙女舞を元にして巫女舞そのものがより芸術性の高い舞へと変革されていったともいわれている。
==地名== *八乙女浦（[[山形県]][[鶴岡市]]由良）：[[蜂子皇子]]を、舞を踊って出迎えた八人の美しい乙女に由来する。 *八乙女洞窟（山形県鶴岡市由良）：明治時代の酒田地震により、崩れた岩で入口が塞がっている。 *八乙女公園（[[秋田県]][[大仙市]]）：八乙女城に由来する。 *[[八乙女 (仙台市)|八乙女]]（[[宮城県]][[仙台市]][[泉区 (仙台市)|泉区]]） *八乙女山（[[富山県]][[南砺市]]）：平成の大合併で新市名を八乙女市にする案もあったが、市議会の多数決により南砺市に決定した。また京都の[[五山送り火|大文字焼き]]に似た八文字焼きという行事が毎年行われている。
==関連項目== *[[巫女]]
{{DEFAULTSORT:やおとめ}} [[category:神道]] [[category:聖職者]] [[Category:神楽]] {{shinto-stub}}
[[ko:야오토메]]

revision.text:
'''八乙女'''（やおとめ）とは、主に[[神楽]]や[[舞]]（いわゆる[[巫女神楽]]・[[巫女舞]]）をもって奉仕する8人の[[巫女]]のこと。'''八社女'''・'''八少女'''・'''八乎止女'''とも書く。また、略して'''八女'''（やめ）とも。
==概要== ただし、人数が8人と定まったのは後世の事であり、古くは「八」の字は複数あるいは多くという意味で使われていたもので、神霊を扱う神聖な処女の意味があったと言われている。また、巫女の群遊の場合には「七」という数字が用いられる事例がある（『[[古事記]]』[[高佐士野]]の説話など）。

八乙女による巫女神楽や巫女舞を「八乙女神楽」・「八乙女舞」と呼ぶ。古くは[[春日大社]]や[[厳島神社]]の八乙女がよく知られていたが、今日では[[美保神社]]や[[金刀比羅宮]]、[[豊国神社 (京都市)|豊国神社]]の八乙女舞も有名になっている。また、[[明治時代]]に政府の方針で巫女舞そのものが危機に瀕した際には、春日大社の八乙女舞を元にして巫女舞そのものがより芸術性の高い舞へと変革されていったともいわれている。
==地名== *八乙女浦（[[山形県]][[鶴岡市]]由良）：[[蜂子皇子]]を、舞を踊って出迎えた八人の美しい乙女に由来する。 *八乙女洞窟（山形県鶴岡市由良）：明治時代の酒田地震により、崩れた岩で入口が塞がっている。 *八乙女公園（[[秋田県]][[大仙市]]）：八乙女城に由来する。 *[[八乙女 (仙台市)|八乙女]]（[[宮城県]][[仙台市]][[泉区 (仙台市)|泉区]]） *八乙女山（[[富山県]][[南砺市]]）：平成の大合併で新市名を八乙女市にする案もあったが、市議会の多数決により南砺市に決定した。また京都の[[五山送り火|大文字送り火]]に似た八文字焼きという行事が毎年行われている。
==関連項目== *[[巫女]]

'''八乙女'''（やおとめ）とは、主に[[神楽]]や[[舞]]（いわゆる[[巫女神楽]]・[[巫女舞]]）をもって奉仕する8人の[[巫女]]のこと。'''八社女'''・'''八少女'''・'''八乎止女'''とも書く。また、略して'''八女'''（やめ）とも。 神功皇后の筑紫行幸啓の折に詠まれたと伝わる神歌に、「八女（やおとめ）は 誰（た）か 八女（やおとめ）そ 天（あめ）に坐す 天若御子（あめわかみこ）の 神の八女（やおとめ）」とあります。 ==概要== ただし、人数が8人と定まったのは後世の事であり、古くは「八」の字は複数あるいは多くという意味で使われていたもので、神霊を扱う神聖な処女の意味があったと言われている。また、巫女の群遊の場合には「七」という数字が用いられる事例がある（『[[古事記]]』[[高佐士野]]の説話など）。
[[古代]]の[[景行天皇]]の[[大嘗祭]]の際に[[天皇]]と[[神々]]に食事を奉仕した巫女に由来するとされている。後には[[神祇官]]において卜定められた[[采女]]がこうした任務にあたった。この影響を受けて他の[[神社]]においても同様の役目の巫女が置かれ、更には神事にも関わるようになったとされている。
八乙女による巫女神楽や巫女舞を「八乙女神楽」・「八乙女舞」と呼ぶ。古くは[[春日大社]]や[[厳島神社]]の八乙女がよく知られていたが、今日では[[美保神社]]や[[金刀比羅宮]]、[[豊国神社 (京都市)|豊国神社]]の八乙女舞も有名になっている。また、[[明治時代]]に政府の方針で巫女舞そのものが危機に瀕した際には、春日大社の八乙女舞を元にして巫女舞そのものがより芸術性の高い舞へと変革されていったともいわれている。
==地名== *八乙女浦（[[山形県]][[鶴岡市]]由良）：[[蜂子皇子]]を、舞を踊って出迎えた八人の美しい乙女に由来する。 *八乙女洞窟（山形県鶴岡市由良）：明治時代の酒田地震により、崩れた岩で入口が塞がっている。 *八乙女公園（[[秋田県]][[大仙市]]）：八乙女城に由来する。 *[[八乙女 (仙台市)|八乙女]]（[[宮城県]][[仙台市]][[泉区 (仙台市)|泉区]]） *[[八乙女 (いすみ市)|八乙女]]([[千葉県]][[いすみ市]], (旧[[夷隅郡]][[夷隅町]])) *八乙女山（[[富山県]][[南砺市]]）：平成の大合併で新市名を八乙女市にする案もあったが、市議会の多数決により南砺市に決定した。また京都の[[五山送り火|大文字送り火]]に似た八文字焼きという行事が毎年行われている。
==関連項目== *[[巫女]]
{{shinto-stub}} {{デフォルトソート:やおとめ}} [[Category:巫女]] [[Category:神楽]] [[Category:名数8|おとめ]]

revision.comment: Created page with "[[File:Sumiyoshi jinja Otaue.jpg|thumb|360px|Sumiyoshi Taisha Otaue Ritual.]] '''Sumiyoshi no Otaue''' (住吉の御田植) is a traditional folk performing art and rice-planting ritual (田楽) associated with the {{ill|Otaue Matsuri|ja|御田植祭}} at {{ill|Sumiyoshi Taisha|ja|住吉大社}} in {{ill|Sumiyoshi-ku, Osaka|ja|住吉区}}, Osaka, Japan. Since the beginning of {{ill|rice cultivation|ja|稲作}} in Japanese history, rituals to honor the {{ill|Ta-no-Kami|j..."

revision.text:
[[File:Sumiyoshi jinja Otaue.jpg|thumb|360px|Sumiyoshi Taisha Otaue Ritual.]] ians, {{ill|yaotome|en}}[[ja:八乙女]], ceremonial women, planters, substitutes, children attendants, warriors in colorful garb, and dancers of the {{ill|Sumiyoshi Odori|en}}[[ja:住吉踊り]], then heads to the rice field.
After sacred water is sprinkled around the field to purify it, rice seedlings are handed over for planting. Planting begins when seedlings are transferred from the planters to substitutes. Throughout the ritual, various performances are offered, including rice-planting dances by the yaotome, mock battles between red and white teams of warriors using bells, drums, and {{ill|conch shells|en}}[[ja:ほら貝]], and performances of Sumiyoshi Odori, in which children dressed as monks dance around a large umbrella held by a leader chanting songs. The dances and rituals aim to honor and enhance the spiritual power of the rice.
On {{ill|February 3, 1979|en}}[[ja:1979年2月3日]], Sumiyoshi no Otaue was designated as an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|en}}[[ja:重要無形民俗文化財]].
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|en}}[[ja:伝統芸能|en]]
== External Links == * [https://www.sumiyoshitaisha.net/events/special.html#anchor01 Otaue (Official Sumiyoshi Taisha website)] Accessed September 22, 2009. * {{Cultural Heritage Online|302|103|住吉の御田植]]

[[Category:Sumiyoshi-no-Otaue Festival forks]]

[[File:Sumiyoshi jinja Otaue.jpg|thumb|360px|Sumiyoshi Taisha Otaue Ritual.]] ians, {{ill|yaotome|en}}[[ja:八乙女]], ceremonial women, planters, substitutes, children attendants, warriors in colorful garb, and dancers of the {{ill|Sumiyoshi Odori|en}}[[ja:住吉踊り]], then heads to the rice field.
After sacred water is sprinkled around the field to purify it, rice seedlings are handed over for planting. Planting begins when seedlings are transferred from the planters to substitutes. Throughout the ritual, various performances are offered, including rice-planting dances by the yaotome, mock battles between red and white teams of warriors using bells, drums, and {{ill|conch shells|en}}[[ja:ほら貝]], and performances of Sumiyoshi Odori, in which children dressed as monks dance around a large umbrella held by a leader chanting songs. The dances and rituals aim to honor and enhance the spiritual power of the rice.
On {{ill|February 3, 1979|en}}[[ja:1979年2月3日]], Sumiyoshi no Otaue was designated as an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|en}}[[ja:重要無形民俗文化財]].

[[File:Sumiyoshi jinja Otaue.jpg|thumb|360px|Sumiyoshi Taisha Otaue Ritual.]] ians, {{ill|yaotome|en}}[[ja:八乙女]], ceremonial women, planters, substitutes, children attendants, warriors in colorful garb, and dancers of the {{ill|Sumiyoshi Odori|en}}[[ja:住吉踊り]], then heads to the rice field.
After sacred water is sprinkled around the field to purify it, rice seedlings are handed over for planting. Planting begins when seedlings are transferred from the planters to substitutes. Throughout the ritual, various performances are offered, including rice-planting dances by the yaotome, mock battles between red and white teams of warriors using bells, drums, and {{ill|conch shells|en}}[[ja:ほら貝]], and performances of Sumiyoshi Odori, in which children dressed as monks dance around a large umbrella held by a leader chanting songs. The dances and rituals aim to honor and enhance the spiritual power of the rice.
On {{ill|February 3, 1979|en}}[[ja:1979年2月3日]], Sumiyoshi no Otaue was designated as an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|en}}[[ja:重要無形民俗文化財]].

[[File:Sumiyoshi jinja Otaue.jpg|thumb|360px|Sumiyoshi Taisha Otaue Ritual.]] [[ja:八乙女]] [[ja:住吉踊り]] [[ja:ほら貝]] Yaotome refers to eight shrine maidens who mainly perform Kagura and dance (known as Miko Kagura and Miko Mai). They are also written as Yasha-me, Yajo, and Yaotome. They are also abbreviated to Yame. A sacred song that is said to have been composed during Empress Jingu 's '''visit''' to '''Tsukushi''' contains '''the lines ,''' "Who are the eight maidens? Yaotome so, the eight maidens of the heavenly God Ame Waka Miko."
== overview == [ edit ] The number of people was fixed at eight in later times, and in ancient times the character "八" (eight) was used to mean multiple or many, and it is said to have had the meaning of a sacred virgin who handled divine spirits. Also, in the case of groups of shrine maidens, the number "七" (seven) is used ( for example , the story of Takasano in the Kojiki ).
It is said to have originated from the shrine maidens who served food to the emperor and the gods during the Daijosai ceremony of the ancient Emperor Keikō . Later, these duties were carried out by Saijo, who were determined by divination at the Jingikan . Influenced by this, shrine maidens with similar roles were placed at other shrines , and they also became involved in Shinto rituals.
The shrine maiden dance and shrine maiden dance performed by the Yaotome are called "Yaoto Kagura" and "Yaotomai." In the past, the Yaotome of Kasuga Taisha and Itsukushima Shrine were well known, but today the Yaotome dances of Miho Shrine , Kotohiragu Shrine , and Toyokuni Shrine are also well known. It is also said that when shrine maiden dance itself was in danger due to government policy during the Meiji period , shrine maiden dance itself was transformed into a more artistic dance based on the Yaotome dance of Kasuga Taisha. [[ja:1979年2月3日]] [[ja:重要無形民俗文化財]] == Related Topics == * {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|en}}[[ja:伝統芸能|en]]

[[File:Sumiyoshi jinja Otaue.jpg|thumb|360px|Sumiyoshi Taisha Otaue Ritual.]] [[ja:八乙女]] [[ja:住吉踊り]] [[ja:ほら貝]] Yaotome refers to eight shrine maidens who mainly perform Kagura and dance (known as Miko Kagura and Miko Mai). They are also written as Yasha-me, Yajo, and Yaotome. They are also abbreviated to Yame. A sacred song that is said to have been composed during Empress Jingu 's '''visit''' to '''Tsukushi''' contains '''the lines ,''' "Who are the eight maidens? Yaotome so, the eight maidens of the heavenly God Ame Waka Miko."
== overview == The number of people was fixed at eight in later times, and in ancient times the character "八" (eight) was used to mean multiple or many, and it is said to have had the meaning of a sacred virgin who handled divine spirits. Also, in the case of groups of shrine maidens, the number "七" (seven) is used ( for example , the story of Takasano in the Kojiki ).
It is said to have originated from the shrine maidens who served food to the emperor and the gods during the [[Daijosai]] ceremony of the ancient [[Emperor Keikō]] . Later, these duties were carried out by Saijo, who were determined by divination at the Jingikan . Influenced by this, shrine maidens with similar roles were placed at other shrines , and they also became involved in Shinto rituals.
The shrine maiden dance and shrine maiden dance performed by the Yaotome are called "Yaoto Kagura" and "Yaotomai." In the past, the Yaotome of Kasuga Taisha and Itsukushima Shrine were well known, but today the Yaotome dances of Miho Shrine , Kotohiragu Shrine , and Toyokuni Shrine are also well known. It is also said that when shrine maiden dance itself was in danger due to government policy during the Meiji period , shrine maiden dance itself was transformed into a more artistic dance based on the Yaotome dance of Kasuga Taisha. [[ja:1979年2月3日]] [[ja:重要無形民俗文化財]] == Related Topics == * {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|en}}[[ja:伝統芸能|en]]

[[File:Sumiyoshi jinja Otaue.jpg|thumb|360px|Sumiyoshi Taisha Otaue Ritual.]] [[ja:八乙女]] [[ja:住吉踊り]] [[ja:ほら貝]] Yaotome refers to eight shrine maidens who mainly perform Kagura and dance (known as Miko Kagura and Miko Mai). They are also written as Yasha-me, Yajo, and Yaotome. They are also abbreviated to Yame. A sacred song that is said to have been composed during Empress Jingu 's '''visit''' to '''Tsukushi''' contains '''the lines ,''' "Who are the eight maidens? Yaotome so, the eight maidens of the heavenly God Ame Waka Miko."
== overview == The number of people was fixed at eight in later times, and in ancient times the character "八" (eight) was used to mean multiple or many, and it is said to have had the meaning of a sacred virgin who handled divine spirits. Also, in the case of groups of shrine maidens, the number "七" (seven) is used ( for example , the story of Takasano in the Kojiki ).
It is said to have originated from the shrine maidens who served food to the emperor and the gods during the [[Daijosai]] ceremony of the ancient [[Emperor Keikō]] . Later, these duties were carried out by Saijo, who were determined by divination at the Jingikan . Influenced by this, shrine maidens with similar roles were placed at other shrines , and they also became involved in Shinto rituals.
The shrine maiden dance and shrine maiden dance performed by the Yaotome are called "Yaoto Kagura" and "Yaotomai." In the past, the Yaotome of Kasuga Taisha and Itsukushima Shrine were well known, but today the Yaotome dances of Miho Shrine , Kotohiragu Shrine , and Toyokuni Shrine are also well known. It is also said that when shrine maiden dance itself was in danger due to government policy during the Meiji period , shrine maiden dance itself was transformed into a more artistic dance based on the Yaotome dance of Kasuga Taisha. [[ja:1979年2月3日]] [[ja:重要無形民俗文化財]] == Related Topics == * {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|en}}[[ja:伝統芸能|en]]

[[File:Sumiyoshi jinja Otaue.jpg|thumb|360px|Sumiyoshi Taisha Otaue Ritual.]] [[ja:八乙女]] [[ja:住吉踊り]] [[ja:ほら貝]] Yaotome refers to eight shrine maidens who mainly perform Kagura and dance (known as Miko Kagura and Miko Mai). They are also written as Yasha-me, Yajo, and Yaotome. They are also abbreviated to Yame. A sacred song that is said to have been composed during Empress Jingu 's '''visit''' to '''Tsukushi''' contains '''the lines ,''' "Who are the eight maidens? Yaotome so, the eight maidens of the heavenly God Ame Waka Miko."
== overview == The number of people was fixed at eight in later times, and in ancient times the character "八" (eight) was used to mean multiple or many, and it is said to have had the meaning of a sacred virgin who handled divine spirits. Also, in the case of groups of shrine maidens, the number "七" (seven) is used ( for example , the story of Takasano in the Kojiki ).
It is said to have originated from the shrine maidens who served food to the emperor and the gods during the [[Daijosai]] ceremony of the ancient [[Emperor Keikō]] . Later, these duties were carried out by Saijo, who were determined by divination at the Jingikan . Influenced by this, shrine maidens with similar roles were placed at other shrines , and they also became involved in Shinto rituals.
The shrine maiden dance and shrine maiden dance performed by the Yaotome are called "Yaoto Kagura" and "Yaotomai." In the past, the Yaotome of Kasuga Taisha and Itsukushima Shrine were well known, but today the Yaotome dances of Miho Shrine , Kotohiragu Shrine , and Toyokuni Shrine are also well known. It is also said that when shrine maiden dance itself was in danger due to government policy during the Meiji period , shrine maiden dance itself was transformed into a more artistic dance based on the Yaotome dance of Kasuga Taisha. [[ja:1979年2月3日]] [[ja:重要無形民俗文化財]] == Related Topics == * {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|en}}[[ja:伝統芸能|en]]

[[Category:Sumiyoshi-no-Otaue Festival forks]] [[Category:Https://ko.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[Category:Yaotome]][[ko:%EC%95%BC%EC%98%A4%ED%86%A0%EB%A9%94]]

[[File:Sumiyoshi jinja Otaue.jpg|thumb|360px|Sumiyoshi Taisha Otaue Ritual.]] [[ja:八乙女]] [[ja:住吉踊り]] [[ja:ほら貝]] Yaotome refers to eight shrine maidens who mainly perform Kagura and dance (known as Miko Kagura and Miko Mai). They are also written as Yasha-me, Yajo, and Yaotome. They are also abbreviated to Yame. A sacred song that is said to have been composed during Empress Jingu 's '''visit''' to '''Tsukushi''' contains '''the lines ,''' "Who are the eight maidens? Yaotome so, the eight maidens of the heavenly God Ame Waka Miko."
== overview == The number of people was fixed at eight in later times, and in ancient times the character "八" (eight) was used to mean multiple or many, and it is said to have had the meaning of a sacred virgin who handled divine spirits. Also, in the case of groups of shrine maidens, the number "七" (seven) is used ( for example , the story of Takasano in the Kojiki ).
It is said to have originated from the shrine maidens who served food to the emperor and the gods during the [[Daijosai]] ceremony of the ancient [[Emperor Keikō]] . Later, these duties were carried out by Saijo, who were determined by divination at the Jingikan . Influenced by this, shrine maidens with similar roles were placed at other shrines , and they also became involved in Shinto rituals.
The shrine maiden dance and shrine maiden dance performed by the Yaotome are called "Yaoto Kagura" and "Yaotomai." In the past, the Yaotome of Kasuga Taisha and Itsukushima Shrine were well known, but today the Yaotome dances of Miho Shrine , Kotohiragu Shrine , and Toyokuni Shrine are also well known. It is also said that when shrine maiden dance itself was in danger due to government policy during the Meiji period , shrine maiden dance itself was transformed into a more artistic dance based on the Yaotome dance of Kasuga Taisha. [[ja:1979年2月3日]] [[ja:重要無形民俗文化財]] == Related Topics == * {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|en}}[[ja:伝統芸能|en]]

Influenced by this, shrine maidens with similar roles were placed at other shrines , and they also became involved in Shinto rituals.
The shrine maiden dance and shrine maiden dance performed by the Yaotome are called "Yaoto Kagura" and "Yaotomai." In the past, the Yaotome of Kasuga Taisha and Itsukushima Shrine were well known, but today the Yaotome dances of Miho Shrine , Kotohiragu Shrine , and Toyokuni Shrine are also well known. It is also said that when shrine maiden dance itself was in danger due to government policy during the Meiji period , shrine maiden dance itself was transformed into a more artistic dance based on the Yaotome dance of Kasuga Taisha. [[ja:1979年2月3日]] [[ja:重要無形民俗文化財]] == Related Topics == * {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|en}}[[ja:伝統芸能|en]]

[[Category:Sumiyoshi-no-Otaue Festival forks]] [[Category:Https://ko.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[Category:Yaotome]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:subst:MAINSPACE]] [[ko:%EC%95%BC%EC%98%A4%ED%86%A0%EB%A9%94]]

[[File:Sumiyoshi jinja Otaue.jpg|thumb|360px|Sumiyoshi Taisha Otaue Ritual.]] [[ja:八乙女]] [[ja:住吉踊り]] [[ja:ほら貝]] Yaotome refers to eight shrine maidens who mainly perform Kagura and dance (known as Miko Kagura and Miko Mai). They are also written as Yasha-me, Yajo, and Yaotome. They are also abbreviated to Yame. A sacred song that is said to have been composed during Empress Jingu 's '''visit''' to '''Tsukushi''' contains '''the lines ,''' "Who are the eight maidens? Yaotome so, the eight maidens of the heavenly God Ame Waka Miko."
== overview == The number of people was fixed at eight in later times, and in ancient times the character "八" (eight) was used to mean multiple or many, and it is said to have had the meaning of a sacred virgin who handled divine spirits. Also, in the case of groups of shrine maidens, the number "七" (seven) is used ( for example , the story of Takasano in the Kojiki ).
It is said to have originated from the shrine maidens who served food to the emperor and the gods during the [[Daijosai]] ceremony of the ancient [[Emperor Keikō]] . Later, these duties were carried out by Saijo, who were determined by divination at the Jingikan . Influenced by this, shrine maidens with similar roles were placed at other shrines , and they also became involved in Shinto rituals.
The shrine maiden dance and shrine maiden dance performed by the Yaotome are called "Yaoto Kagura" and "Yaotomai." In the past, the Yaotome of Kasuga Taisha and Itsukushima Shrine were well known, but today the Yaotome dances of Miho Shrine , Kotohiragu Shrine , and Toyokuni Shrine are also well known. It is also said that when shrine maiden dance itself was in danger due to government policy during the Meiji period , shrine maiden dance itself was transformed into a more artistic dance based on the Yaotome dance of Kasuga Taisha. [[ja:1979年2月3日]] [[ja:重要無形民俗文化財]] == Related Topics == * {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|en}}[[ja:伝統芸能|en]]

[[File:Sumiyoshi jinja Otaue.jpg|thumb|360px|Sumiyoshi Taisha Otaue Ritual.]] [[ja:八乙女]] [[ja:住吉踊り]] [[ja:ほら貝]] Yaotome refers to eight shrine maidens who mainly perform Kagura and dance (known as Miko Kagura and Miko Mai). They are also written as Yasha-me, Yajo, and Yaotome. They are also abbreviated to Yame. A sacred song that is said to have been composed during Empress Jingu 's '''visit''' to '''Tsukushi''' contains '''the lines ,''' "Who are the eight maidens? Yaotome so, the eight maidens of the heavenly God Ame Waka Miko."
== overview == The number of people was fixed at eight in later times, and in ancient times the character "八" (eight) was used to mean multiple or many, and it is said to have had the meaning of a sacred virgin who handled divine spirits. Also, in the case of groups of shrine maidens, the number "七" (seven) is used ( for example , the story of Takasano in the Kojiki ).
It is said to have originated from the shrine maidens who served food to the emperor and the gods during the [[Daijosai]] ceremony of the ancient [[Emperor Keikō]] . Later, these duties were carried out by Saijo, who were determined by divination at the Jingikan . Influenced by this, shrine maidens with similar roles were placed at other shrines , and they also became involved in Shinto rituals.
The shrine maiden dance and shrine maiden dance performed by the Yaotome are called "Yaoto Kagura" and "Yaotomai." In the past, the Yaotome of Kasuga Taisha and Itsukushima Shrine were well known, but today the Yaotome dances of Miho Shrine , Kotohiragu Shrine , and Toyokuni Shrine are also well known. It is also said that when shrine maiden dance itself was in danger due to government policy during the Meiji period , shrine maiden dance itself was transformed into a more artistic dance based on the Yaotome dance of Kasuga Taisha. [[ja:1979年2月3日]] [[ja:重要無形民俗文化財]] == Related Topics == * {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|en}}[[ja:伝統芸能|en]]

[[Category:Sumiyoshi-no-Otaue Festival forks]] [[Category:Https://ko.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[Category:Yaotome]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:Subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Yaotome}][[Category:qq]] [[ko:%EC%95%BC%EC%98%A4%ED%86%A0%EB%A9%94]]

[[File:Sumiyoshi jinja Otaue.jpg|thumb|360px|Sumiyoshi Taisha Otaue Ritual.]] [[ja:八乙女]] [[ja:住吉踊り]] [[ja:ほら貝]] Yaotome refers to eight shrine maidens who mainly perform Kagura and dance (known as Miko Kagura and Miko Mai). They are also written as Yasha-me, Yajo, and Yaotome. They are also abbreviated to Yame. A sacred song that is said to have been composed during Empress Jingu 's '''visit''' to '''Tsukushi''' contains '''the lines ,''' "Who are the eight maidens? Yaotome so, the eight maidens of the heavenly God Ame Waka Miko."
== overview == The number of people was fixed at eight in later times, and in ancient times the character "八" (eight) was used to mean multiple or many, and it is said to have had the meaning of a sacred virgin who handled divine spirits. Also, in the case of groups of shrine maidens, the number "七" (seven) is used ( for example , the story of Takasano in the Kojiki ).
It is said to have originated from the shrine maidens who served food to the emperor and the gods during the [[Daijosai]] ceremony of the ancient [[Emperor Keikō]] . Later, these duties were carried out by Saijo, who were determined by divination at the Jingikan . Influenced by this, shrine maidens with similar roles were placed at other shrines , and they also became involved in Shinto rituals.
The shrine maiden dance and shrine maiden dance performed by the Yaotome are called "Yaoto Kagura" and "Yaotomai." In the past, the Yaotome of Kasuga Taisha and Itsukushima Shrine were well known, but today the Yaotome dances of Miho Shrine , Kotohiragu Shrine , and Toyokuni Shrine are also well known. It is also said that when shrine maiden dance itself was in danger due to government policy during the Meiji period , shrine maiden dance itself was transformed into a more artistic dance based on the Yaotome dance of Kasuga Taisha. [[ja:1979年2月3日]] [[ja:重要無形民俗文化財]] == Related Topics == * {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|en}}[[ja:伝統芸能|en]]
== External Links == * [https://www.sumiyoshitaisha.net/events/special.html#anchor01 Otaue (Official Sumiyoshi Taisha website)] Accessed September 22, 2009. * {{Cultural Heritage Online|302|103|住吉の御田植]]

[[File:Sumiyoshi jinja Otaue.jpg|thumb|360px|Sumiyoshi Taisha Otaue Ritual.]] [[ja:八乙女]] [[ja:住吉踊り]] [[ja:ほら貝]] Yaotome refers to eight shrine maidens who mainly perform Kagura and dance (known as Miko Kagura and Miko Mai). They are also written as Yasha-me, Yajo, and Yaotome. They are also abbreviated to Yame. A sacred song that is said to have been composed during Empress Jingu 's '''visit''' to '''Tsukushi''' contains '''the lines ,''' "Who are the eight maidens? Yaotome so, the eight maidens of the heavenly God Ame Waka Miko."
== overview == The number of people was fixed at eight in later times, and in ancient times the character "八" (eight) was used to mean multiple or many, and it is said to have had the meaning of a sacred virgin who handled divine spirits. Also, in the case of groups of shrine maidens, the number "七" (seven) is used ( for example , the story of Takasano in the Kojiki ).
It is said to have originated from the shrine maidens who served food to the emperor and the gods during the [[Daijosai]] ceremony of the ancient [[Emperor Keikō]] . Later, these duties were carried out by Saijo, who were determined by divination at the Jingikan . Influenced by this, shrine maidens with similar roles were placed at other shrines , and they also became involved in Shinto rituals.
The shrine maiden dance and shrine maiden dance performed by the Yaotome are called "Yaoto Kagura" and "Yaotomai." In the past, the Yaotome of Kasuga Taisha and Itsukushima Shrine were well known, but today the Yaotome dances of Miho Shrine , Kotohiragu Shrine , and Toyokuni Shrine are also well known. It is also said that when shrine maiden dance itself was in danger due to government policy during the Meiji period , shrine maiden dance itself was transformed into a more artistic dance based on the Yaotome dance of Kasuga Taisha. [[ja:1979年2月3日]] [[ja:重要無形民俗文化財]] == Related Topics == * {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|en}}[[ja:伝統芸能|en]]

[[File:Sumiyoshi jinja Otaue.jpg|thumb|360px|Sumiyoshi Taisha Otaue Ritual.]] [[ja:八乙女]] [[ja:住吉踊り]] [[ja:ほら貝]] Yaotome refers to eight shrine maidens who mainly perform Kagura and dance (known as Miko Kagura and Miko Mai). They are also written as Yasha-me, Yajo, and Yaotome. They are also abbreviated to Yame. A sacred song that is said to have been composed during Empress Jingu 's '''visit''' to '''Tsukushi''' contains '''the lines ,''' "Who are the eight maidens? Yaotome so, the eight maidens of the heavenly God Ame Waka Miko."
== overview == The number of people was fixed at eight in later times, and in ancient times the character "八" (eight) was used to mean multiple or many, and it is said to have had the meaning of a sacred virgin who handled divine spirits. Also, in the case of groups of shrine maidens, the number "七" (seven) is used ( for example , the story of Takasano in the Kojiki ).
It is said to have originated from the shrine maidens who served food to the emperor and the gods during the [[Daijosai]] ceremony of the ancient [[Emperor Keikō]] . Later, these duties were carried out by Saijo, who were determined by divination at the Jingikan . Influenced by this, shrine maidens with similar roles were placed at other shrines , and they also became involved in Shinto rituals.
The shrine maiden dance and shrine maiden dance performed by the Yaotome are called "Yaoto Kagura" and "Yaotomai." In the past, the Yaotome of Kasuga Taisha and Itsukushima Shrine were well known, but today the Yaotome dances of Miho Shrine , Kotohiragu Shrine , and Toyokuni Shrine are also well known. It is also said that when shrine maiden dance itself was in danger due to government policy during the Meiji period , shrine maiden dance itself was transformed into a more artistic dance based on the Yaotome dance of Kasuga Taisha. [[ja:1979年2月3日]] [[ja:重要無形民俗文化財]] == Related Topics == * {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|en}}[[ja:伝統芸能|en]]

[[File:Sumiyoshi jinja Otaue.jpg|thumb|360px|Sumiyoshi Taisha Otaue Ritual.]] [[ja:八乙女]] [[ja:住吉踊り]] [[ja:ほら貝]] Yaotome refers to eight shrine maidens who mainly perform Kagura and dance (known as Miko Kagura and Miko Mai). They are also written as Yasha-me, Yajo, and Yaotome. They are also abbreviated to Yame. A sacred song that is said to have been composed during Empress Jingu 's '''visit''' to '''Tsukushi''' contains '''the lines ,''' "Who are the eight maidens? Yaotome so, the eight maidens of the heavenly God Ame Waka Miko."
== overview == The number of people was fixed at eight in later times, and in ancient times the character "八" (eight) was used to mean multiple or many, and it is said to have had the meaning of a sacred virgin who handled divine spirits. Also, in the case of groups of shrine maidens, the number "七" (seven) is used ( for example , the story of Takasano in the Kojiki ).
It is said to have originated from the shrine maidens who served food to the emperor and the gods during the [[Daijosai]] ceremony of the ancient [[Emperor Keikō]] . Later, these duties were carried out by Saijo, who were determined by divination at the Jingikan . Influenced by this, shrine maidens with similar roles were placed at other shrines , and they also became involved in Shinto rituals.
The shrine maiden dance and shrine maiden dance performed by the Yaotome are called "Yaoto Kagura" and "Yaotomai." In the past, the Yaotome of Kasuga Taisha and Itsukushima Shrine were well known, but today the Yaotome dances of Miho Shrine , Kotohiragu Shrine , and Toyokuni Shrine are also well known. It is also said that when shrine maiden dance itself was in danger due to government policy during the Meiji period , shrine maiden dance itself was transformed into a more artistic dance based on the Yaotome dance of Kasuga Taisha. [[ja:1979年2月3日]] [[ja:重要無形民俗文化財]] == Related Topics == * {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|en}}[[ja:伝統芸能|en]]

[[File:Sumiyoshi jinja Otaue.jpg|thumb|360px|Sumiyoshi Taisha Otaue Ritual.]] [[ja:八乙女]] [[ja:住吉踊り]] [[ja:ほら貝]] Yaotome refers to eight shrine maidens who mainly perform Kagura and dance (known as Miko Kagura and Miko Mai). They are also written as Yasha-me, Yajo, and Yaotome. They are also abbreviated to Yame. A sacred song that is said to have been composed during Empress Jingu 's '''visit''' to '''Tsukushi''' contains '''the lines ,''' "Who are the eight maidens? Yaotome so, the eight maidens of the heavenly God Ame Waka Miko."
== overview == The number of people was fixed at eight in later times, and in ancient times the character "八" (eight) was used to mean multiple or many, and it is said to have had the meaning of a sacred virgin who handled divine spirits. Also, in the case of groups of shrine maidens, the number "七" (seven) is used ( for example , the story of Takasano in the Kojiki ).
It is said to have originated from the shrine maidens who served food to the emperor and the gods during the [[Daijosai]] ceremony of the ancient [[Emperor Keikō]] . Later, these duties were carried out by Saijo, who were determined by divination at the Jingikan . Influenced by this, shrine maidens with similar roles were placed at other shrines , and they also became involved in Shinto rituals.
The shrine maiden dance and shrine maiden dance performed by the Yaotome are called "Yaoto Kagura" and "Yaotomai." In the past, the Yaotome of Kasuga Taisha and Itsukushima Shrine were well known, but today the Yaotome dances of Miho Shrine , Kotohiragu Shrine , and Toyokuni Shrine are also well known. It is also said that when shrine maiden dance itself was in danger due to government policy during the Meiji period , shrine maiden dance itself was transformed into a more artistic dance based on the Yaotome dance of Kasuga Taisha. [[ja:1979年2月3日]] [[ja:重要無形民俗文化財]] == Related Topics == * {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|en}}[[ja:伝統芸能|en]]

[[File:Sumiyoshi jinja Otaue.jpg|thumb|360px|Sumiyoshi Taisha Otaue Ritual.]] [[ja:八乙女]] [[ja:住吉踊り]] [[ja:ほら貝]] Yaotome refers to eight shrine maidens who mainly perform Kagura and dance (known as Miko Kagura and Miko Mai). They are also written as Yasha-me, Yajo, and Yaotome. They are also abbreviated to Yame. A sacred song that is said to have been composed during Empress Jingu 's '''visit''' to '''Tsukushi''' contains '''the lines ,''' "Who are the eight maidens? Yaotome so, the eight maidens of the heavenly God Ame Waka Miko."
== overview == The number of people was fixed at eight in later times, and in ancient times the character "八" (eight) was used to mean multiple or many, and it is said to have had the meaning of a sacred virgin who handled divine spirits. Also, in the case of groups of shrine maidens, the number "七" (seven) is used ( for example , the story of Takasano in the Kojiki ).
It is said to have originated from the shrine maidens who served food to the emperor and the gods during the [[Daijosai]] ceremony of the ancient [[Emperor Keikō]] . Later, these duties were carried out by Saijo, who were determined by divination at the Jingikan . Influenced by this, shrine maidens with similar roles were placed at other shrines , and they also became involved in Shinto rituals.
The shrine maiden dance and shrine maiden dance performed by the Yaotome are called "Yaoto Kagura" and "Yaotomai." In the past, the Yaotome of Kasuga Taisha and Itsukushima Shrine were well known, but today the Yaotome dances of Miho Shrine , Kotohiragu Shrine , and Toyokuni Shrine are also well known. It is also said that when shrine maiden dance itself was in danger due to government policy during the Meiji period , shrine maiden dance itself was transformed into a more artistic dance based on the Yaotome dance of Kasuga Taisha. [[ja:1979年2月3日]] [[ja:重要無形民俗文化財]] == Related Topics == * {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|en}}[[ja:伝統芸能|en]]

[[File:Sumiyoshi jinja Otaue.jpg|thumb|360px|Sumiyoshi Taisha Otaue Ritual.]] [[ja:八乙女]] [[ja:住吉踊り]] [[ja:ほら貝]] Yaotome refers to eight shrine maidens who mainly perform Kagura and dance (known as Miko Kagura and Miko Mai). They are also written as Yasha-me, Yajo, and Yaotome. They are also abbreviated to Yame. A sacred song that is said to have been composed during Empress Jingu 's '''visit''' to '''Tsukushi''' contains '''the lines ,''' "Who are the eight maidens? Yaotome so, the eight maidens of the heavenly God Ame Waka Miko."
== overview == The number of people was fixed at eight in later times, and in ancient times the character "八" (eight) was used to mean multiple or many, and it is said to have had the meaning of a sacred virgin who handled divine spirits. Also, in the case of groups of shrine maidens, the number "七" (seven) is used ( for example , the story of Takasano in the Kojiki ).
It is said to have originated from the shrine maidens who served food to the emperor and the gods during the [[Daijosai]] ceremony of the ancient [[Emperor Keikō]] . Later, these duties were carried out by Saijo, who were determined by divination at the Jingikan . Influenced by this, shrine maidens with similar roles were placed at other shrines , and they also became involved in Shinto rituals.
The shrine maiden dance and shrine maiden dance performed by the Yaotome are called "Yaoto Kagura" and "Yaotomai." In the past, the Yaotome of Kasuga Taisha and Itsukushima Shrine were well known, but today the Yaotome dances of Miho Shrine , Kotohiragu Shrine , and Toyokuni Shrine are also well known. It is also said that when shrine maiden dance itself was in danger due to government policy during the Meiji period , shrine maiden dance itself was transformed into a more artistic dance based on the Yaotome dance of Kasuga Taisha. [[ja:1979年2月3日]] [[ja:重要無形民俗文化財]] == Related Topics == * {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|en}}[[ja:伝統芸能|en]]
== External Links == * [https://www.sumiyoshitaisha.net/events/special.html#anchor01 Otaue (Official Sumiyoshi Taisha website)] Accessed September 22, 2009. * {{Cultural Heritage Online|302|103|住吉の御田植]]

[[File:Sumiyoshi jinja Otaue.jpg|thumb|360px|Sumiyoshi Taisha Otaue Ritual.]] [[ja:八乙女]] [[ja:住吉踊り]] [[ja:ほら貝]] Yaotome refers to eight shrine maidens who mainly perform Kagura and dance (known as Miko Kagura and Miko Mai). They are also written as Yasha-me, Yajo, and Yaotome. They are also abbreviated to Yame. A sacred song that is said to have been composed during Empress Jingu 's '''visit''' to '''Tsukushi''' contains '''the lines ,''' "Who are the eight maidens? Yaotome so, the eight maidens of the heavenly God Ame Waka Miko."
== overview == The number of people was fixed at eight in later times, and in ancient times the character "八" (eight) was used to mean multiple or many, and it is said to have had the meaning of a sacred virgin who handled divine spirits. Also, in the case of groups of shrine maidens, the number "七" (seven) is used ( for example , the story of Takasano in the Kojiki ).
It is said to have originated from the shrine maidens who served food to the emperor and the gods during the [[Daijosai]] ceremony of the ancient [[Emperor Keikō]] . Later, these duties were carried out by Saijo, who were determined by divination at the Jingikan . Influenced by this, shrine maidens with similar roles were placed at other shrines , and they also became involved in Shinto rituals.
The shrine maiden dance and shrine maiden dance performed by the Yaotome are called "Yaoto Kagura" and "Yaotomai." In the past, the Yaotome of Kasuga Taisha and Itsukushima Shrine were well known, but today the Yaotome dances of Miho Shrine , Kotohiragu Shrine , and Toyokuni Shrine are also well known. It is also said that when shrine maiden dance itself was in danger due to government policy during the Meiji period , shrine maiden dance itself was transformed into a more artistic dance based on the Yaotome dance of Kasuga Taisha. [[ja:1979年2月3日]] [[ja:重要無形民俗文化財]] == See Also == * {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|en}}[[ja:伝統芸能|en]]

[[File:Sumiyoshi jinja Otaue.jpg|thumb|360px|Sumiyoshi Taisha Otaue Ritual.]] [[ja:八乙女]] [[ja:住吉踊り]] [[ja:ほら貝]] Yaotome refers to eight shrine maidens who mainly perform Kagura and dance (known as Miko Kagura and Miko Mai). They are also written as Yasha-me, Yajo, and Yaotome. They are also abbreviated to Yame. A sacred song that is said to have been composed during Empress Jingu 's '''visit''' to '''Tsukushi''' contains '''the lines ,''' "Who are the eight maidens? Yaotome so, the eight maidens of the heavenly God Ame Waka Miko."
== overview == The number of people was fixed at eight in later times, and in ancient times the character "八" (eight) was used to mean multiple or many, and it is said to have had the meaning of a sacred virgin who handled divine spirits. Also, in the case of groups of shrine maidens, the number "七" (seven) is used ( for example , the story of Takasano in the Kojiki ).
It is said to have originated from the shrine maidens who served food to the emperor and the gods during the [[Daijosai]] ceremony of the ancient [[Emperor Keikō]] . Later, these duties were carried out by Saijo, who were determined by divination at the Jingikan . Influenced by this, shrine maidens with similar roles were placed at other shrines , and they also became involved in Shinto rituals.
The shrine maiden dance and shrine maiden dance performed by the Yaotome are called "Yaoto Kagura" and "Yaotomai." In the past, the Yaotome of Kasuga Taisha and Itsukushima Shrine were well known, but today the Yaotome dances of Miho Shrine , Kotohiragu Shrine , and Toyokuni Shrine are also well known. It is also said that when shrine maiden dance itself was in danger due to government policy during the Meiji period , shrine maiden dance itself was transformed into a more artistic dance based on the Yaotome dance of Kasuga Taisha. [[ja:1979年2月3日]] [[ja:重要無形民俗文化財]] == See Also == * {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|en}}[[ja:伝統芸能|en]]

[[File:Sumiyoshi jinja Otaue.jpg|thumb|360px|Sumiyoshi Taisha Otaue Ritual.]] [[ja:八乙女]] [[ja:住吉踊り]] [[ja:ほら貝]] Yaotome refers to eight shrine maidens who mainly perform Kagura and dance (known as Miko Kagura and Miko Mai). They are also written as Yasha-me, Yajo, and Yaotome. They are also abbreviated to Yame. A sacred song that is said to have been composed during Empress Jingu 's '''visit''' to '''Tsukushi''' contains '''the lines ,''' "Who are the eight maidens? Yaotome so, the eight maidens of the heavenly God Ame Waka Miko."
== overview == The number of people was fixed at eight in later times, and in ancient times the character "八" (eight) was used to mean multiple or many, and it is said to have had the meaning of a sacred virgin who handled divine spirits. Also, in the case of groups of shrine maidens, the number "七" (seven) is used ( for example , the story of Takasano in the Kojiki ).
It is said to have originated from the shrine maidens who served food to the emperor and the gods during the [[Daijosai]] ceremony of the ancient [[Emperor Keikō]] . Later, these duties were carried out by Saijo, who were determined by divination at the Jingikan . Influenced by this, shrine maidens with similar roles were placed at other shrines , and they also became involved in Shinto rituals.
The shrine maiden dance and shrine maiden dance performed by the Yaotome are called "Yaoto Kagura" and "Yaotomai." In the past, the Yaotome of Kasuga Taisha and Itsukushima Shrine were well known, but today the Yaotome dances of Miho Shrine , Kotohiragu Shrine , and Toyokuni Shrine are also well known. It is also said that when shrine maiden dance itself was in danger due to government policy during the Meiji period , shrine maiden dance itself was transformed into a more artistic dance based on the Yaotome dance of Kasuga Taisha. [[ja:1979年2月3日]] [[ja:重要無形民俗文化財]] == See Also == * {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|en}}[[ja:伝統芸能|en]]
== External Links == * [https://www.sumiyoshitaisha.net/events/special.html#anchor01 Otaue (Official Sumiyoshi Taisha website)] Accessed September 22, 2009. * {{Cultural Heritage Online|302|103|住吉の御田植]]

revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:Bumping to try to fix the sexagenary stuff]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
[[File:Sumiyoshi jinja Otaue.jpg|thumb|360px|Sumiyoshi Taisha Otaue Ritual.]] [[ja:八乙女]] [[ja:住吉踊り]] [[ja:ほら貝]] Yaotome refers to eight shrine maidens who mainly perform Kagura and dance (known as Miko Kagura and Miko Mai). They are also written as Yasha-me, Yajo, and Yaotome. They are also abbreviated to Yame. A sacred song that is said to have been composed during Empress Jingu 's '''visit''' to '''Tsukushi''' contains '''the lines ,''' "Who are the eight maidens? Yaotome so, the eight maidens of the heavenly God Ame Waka Miko."
== overview == The number of people was fixed at eight in later times, and in ancient times the character "八" (eight) was used to mean multiple or many, and it is said to have had the meaning of a sacred virgin who handled divine spirits. Also, in the case of groups of shrine maidens, the number "七" (seven) is used ( for example , the story of Takasano in the Kojiki ).
It is said to have originated from the shrine maidens who served food to the emperor and the gods during the [[Daijosai]] ceremony of the ancient [[Emperor Keikō]] . Later, these duties were carried out by Saijo, who were determined by divination at the Jingikan . Influenced by this, shrine maidens with similar roles were placed at other shrines , and they also became involved in Shinto rituals.
The shrine maiden dance and shrine maiden dance performed by the Yaotome are called "Yaoto Kagura" and "Yaotomai." In the past, the Yaotome of Kasuga Taisha and Itsukushima Shrine were well known, but today the Yaotome dances of Miho Shrine , Kotohiragu Shrine , and Toyokuni Shrine are also well known. It is also said that when shrine maiden dance itself was in danger due to government policy during the Meiji period , shrine maiden dance itself was transformed into a more artistic dance based on the Yaotome dance of Kasuga Taisha. [[ja:1979年2月3日]] [[ja:重要無形民俗文化財]] == See Also == * {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|en}}[[ja:伝統芸能|en]]
== External Links == * [https://www.sumiyoshitaisha.net/events/special.html#anchor01 Otaue (Official Sumiyoshi Taisha website)] Accessed September 22, 2009. * {{Cultural Heritage Online|302|103|住吉の御田植]]

revision.text:
[[File:Sumiyoshi jinja Otaue.jpg|thumb|360px|Sumiyoshi Taisha Otaue Ritual.]] [[ja:八乙女]] [[ja:住吉踊り]] [[ja:ほら貝]] Yaotome refers to eight shrine maidens who mainly perform Kagura and dance (known as Miko Kagura and Miko Mai). They are also written as Yasha-me, Yajo, and Yaotome. They are also abbreviated to Yame. A sacred song that is said to have been composed during Empress Jingu 's '''visit''' to '''Tsukushi''' contains '''the lines ,''' "Who are the eight maidens? Yaotome so, the eight maidens of the heavenly God Ame Waka Miko."
== overview == The number of people was fixed at eight in later times, and in ancient times the character "八" (eight) was used to mean multiple or many, and it is said to have had the meaning of a sacred virgin who handled divine spirits. Also, in the case of groups of shrine maidens, the number "七" (seven) is used ( for example , the story of Takasano in the Kojiki ).
It is said to have originated from the shrine maidens who served food to the emperor and the gods during the [[Daijosai]] ceremony of the ancient [[Emperor Keikō]] . Later, these duties were carried out by Saijo, who were determined by divination at the Jingikan . Influenced by this, shrine maidens with similar roles were placed at other shrines , and they also became involved in Shinto rituals.
The shrine maiden dance and shrine maiden dance performed by the Yaotome are called "Yaoto Kagura" and "Yaotomai." In the past, the Yaotome of Kasuga Taisha and Itsukushima Shrine were well known, but today the Yaotome dances of Miho Shrine , Kotohiragu Shrine , and Toyokuni Shrine are also well known. It is also said that when shrine maiden dance itself was in danger due to government policy during the Meiji period , shrine maiden dance itself was transformed into a more artistic dance based on the Yaotome dance of Kasuga Taisha. [[ja:1979年2月3日]] [[ja:重要無形民俗文化財]] == See Also == * {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|en}}[[ja:伝統芸能|en]]
== External Links == * [https://www.sumiyoshitaisha.net/events/special.html#anchor01 Otaue (Official Sumiyoshi Taisha website)] Accessed September 22, 2009. * {{Cultural Heritage Online|302|103|住吉の御田植]]

[[File:Sumiyoshi jinja Otaue.jpg|thumb|360px|Sumiyoshi Taisha Otaue Ritual.]] [[ja:八乙女]] [[ja:住吉踊り]] [[ja:ほら貝]] Yaotome refers to eight shrine maidens who mainly perform Kagura and dance (known as Miko Kagura and Miko Mai). They are also written as Yasha-me, Yajo, and Yaotome. They are also abbreviated to Yame. A sacred song that is said to have been composed during Empress Jingu 's '''visit''' to '''Tsukushi''' contains '''the lines ,''' "Who are the eight maidens? Yaotome so, the eight maidens of the heavenly God Ame Waka Miko."
== overview == The number of people was fixed at eight in later times, and in ancient times the character "八" (eight) was used to mean multiple or many, and it is said to have had the meaning of a sacred virgin who handled divine spirits. Also, in the case of groups of shrine maidens, the number "七" (seven) is used ( for example , the story of Takasano in the Kojiki ).
It is said to have originated from the shrine maidens who served food to the emperor and the gods during the [[Daijosai]] ceremony of the ancient [[Emperor Keikō]] . Later, these duties were carried out by Saijo, who were determined by divination at the Jingikan . Influenced by this, shrine maidens with similar roles were placed at other shrines , and they also became involved in Shinto rituals.
The shrine maiden dance and shrine maiden dance performed by the Yaotome are called "Yaoto Kagura" and "Yaotomai." In the past, the Yaotome of Kasuga Taisha and Itsukushima Shrine were well known, but today the Yaotome dances of Miho Shrine , Kotohiragu Shrine , and Toyokuni Shrine are also well known. It is also said that when shrine maiden dance itself was in danger due to government policy during the Meiji period , shrine maiden dance itself was transformed into a more artistic dance based on the Yaotome dance of Kasuga Taisha. [[ja:1979年2月3日]] [[ja:重要無形民俗文化財]] == See Also == * {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|en}}[[ja:伝統芸能|en]]

[[File:Sumiyoshi jinja Otaue.jpg|thumb|360px|Sumiyoshi Taisha Otaue Ritual.]] [[ja:八乙女]] [[ja:住吉踊り]] [[ja:ほら貝]] Yaotome refers to eight shrine maidens who mainly perform Kagura and dance (known as Miko Kagura and Miko Mai). They are also written as Yasha-me, Yajo, and Yaotome. They are also abbreviated to Yame. A sacred song that is said to have been composed during Empress Jingu 's '''visit''' to '''Tsukushi''' contains '''the lines ,''' "Who are the eight maidens? Yaotome so, the eight maidens of the heavenly God Ame Waka Miko."
== overview == The number of people was fixed at eight in later times, and in ancient times the character "八" (eight) was used to mean multiple or many, and it is said to have had the meaning of a sacred virgin who handled divine spirits. Also, in the case of groups of shrine maidens, the number "七" (seven) is used ( for example , the story of Takasano in the Kojiki ).
It is said to have originated from the shrine maidens who served food to the emperor and the gods during the [[Daijosai]] ceremony of the ancient [[Emperor Keikō]] . Later, these duties were carried out by Saijo, who were determined by divination at the Jingikan . Influenced by this, shrine maidens with similar roles were placed at other shrines , and they also became involved in Shinto rituals.
The shrine maiden dance and shrine maiden dance performed by the Yaotome are called "Yaoto Kagura" and "Yaotomai." In the past, the Yaotome of Kasuga Taisha and Itsukushima Shrine were well known, but today the Yaotome dances of Miho Shrine , Kotohiragu Shrine , and Toyokuni Shrine are also well known. It is also said that when shrine maiden dance itself was in danger due to government policy during the Meiji period , shrine maiden dance itself was transformed into a more artistic dance based on the Yaotome dance of Kasuga Taisha. [[ja:1979年2月3日]] [[ja:重要無形民俗文化財]] == See Also == * {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|en}}[[ja:伝統芸能|en]]

[[File:Sumiyoshi jinja Otaue.jpg|thumb|360px|Sumiyoshi Taisha Otaue Ritual.]] [[ja:八乙女]] [[ja:住吉踊り]] [[ja:ほら貝]] Yaotome refers to eight shrine maidens who mainly perform Kagura and dance (known as Miko Kagura and Miko Mai). They are also written as Yasha-me, Yajo, and Yaotome. They are also abbreviated to Yame. A sacred song that is said to have been composed during Empress Jingu 's '''visit''' to '''Tsukushi''' contains '''the lines ,''' "Who are the eight maidens? Yaotome so, the eight maidens of the heavenly God Ame Waka Miko."
== overview == The number of people was fixed at eight in later times, and in ancient times the character "八" (eight) was used to mean multiple or many, and it is said to have had the meaning of a sacred virgin who handled divine spirits. Also, in the case of groups of shrine maidens, the number "七" (seven) is used ( for example , the story of Takasano in the Kojiki ).
It is said to have originated from the shrine maidens who served food to the emperor and the gods during the [[Daijosai]] ceremony of the ancient [[Emperor Keikō]] . Later, these duties were carried out by Saijo, who were determined by divination at the Jingikan . Influenced by this, shrine maidens with similar roles were placed at other shrines , and they also became involved in Shinto rituals.
The shrine maiden dance and shrine maiden dance performed by the Yaotome are called "Yaoto Kagura" and "Yaotomai." In the past, the Yaotome of Kasuga Taisha and Itsukushima Shrine were well known, but today the Yaotome dances of Miho Shrine , Kotohiragu Shrine , and Toyokuni Shrine are also well known. It is also said that when shrine maiden dance itself was in danger due to government policy during the Meiji period , shrine maiden dance itself was transformed into a more artistic dance based on the Yaotome dance of Kasuga Taisha. [[ja:1979年2月3日]] [[ja:重要無形民俗文化財]] == See Also == * {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|en}}[[ja:伝統芸能|en]]

In the past, the Yaotome of Kasuga Taisha and Itsukushima Shrine were well known, but today the Yaotome dances of Miho Shrine , Kotohiragu Shrine , and Toyokuni Shrine are also well known. It is also said that when shrine maiden dance itself was in danger due to government policy during the Meiji period , shrine maiden dance itself was transformed into a more artistic dance based on the Yaotome dance of Kasuga Taisha. [[ja:1979年2月3日]] [[ja:重要無形民俗文化財]] == See Also == * {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|en}}[[ja:伝統芸能|en]]

revision.comment: Moving from [[Category:Qq close]] to [[Category:New uncategorized original categories]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
[[File:Sumiyoshi jinja Otaue.jpg|thumb|360px|Sumiyoshi Taisha Otaue Ritual.]] [[ja:八乙女]] [[ja:住吉踊り]] [[ja:ほら貝]] Yaotome refers to eight shrine maidens who mainly perform Kagura and dance (known as Miko Kagura and Miko Mai). They are also written as Yasha-me, Yajo, and Yaotome. They are also abbreviated to Yame. A sacred song that is said to have been composed during Empress Jingu 's '''visit''' to '''Tsukushi''' contains '''the lines ,''' "Who are the eight maidens? Yaotome so, the eight maidens of the heavenly God Ame Waka Miko."
== overview == The number of people was fixed at eight in later times, and in ancient times the character "八" (eight) was used to mean multiple or many, and it is said to have had the meaning of a sacred virgin who handled divine spirits. Also, in the case of groups of shrine maidens, the number "七" (seven) is used ( for example , the story of Takasano in the Kojiki ).
It is said to have originated from the shrine maidens who served food to the emperor and the gods during the [[Daijosai]] ceremony of the ancient [[Emperor Keikō]] . Later, these duties were carried out by Saijo, who were determined by divination at the Jingikan . Influenced by this, shrine maidens with similar roles were placed at other shrines , and they also became involved in Shinto rituals.
The shrine maiden dance and shrine maiden dance performed by the Yaotome are called "Yaoto Kagura" and "Yaotomai." In the past, the Yaotome of Kasuga Taisha and Itsukushima Shrine were well known, but today the Yaotome dances of Miho Shrine , Kotohiragu Shrine , and Toyokuni Shrine are also well known. It is also said that when shrine maiden dance itself was in danger due to government policy during the Meiji period , shrine maiden dance itself was transformed into a more artistic dance based on the Yaotome dance of Kasuga Taisha. [[ja:1979年2月3日]] [[ja:重要無形民俗文化財]] == See Also == * {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|en}}[[ja:伝統芸能|en]]

[[File:Sumiyoshi jinja Otaue.jpg|thumb|360px|Sumiyoshi Taisha Otaue Ritual.]] [[ja:八乙女]] [[ja:住吉踊り]] [[ja:ほら貝]] Yaotome refers to eight shrine maidens who mainly perform Kagura and dance (known as Miko Kagura and Miko Mai). They are also written as Yasha-me, Yajo, and Yaotome. They are also abbreviated to Yame. A sacred song that is said to have been composed during Empress Jingu 's '''visit''' to '''Tsukushi''' contains '''the lines ,''' "Who are the eight maidens? Yaotome so, the eight maidens of the heavenly God Ame Waka Miko."
== overview == The number of people was fixed at eight in later times, and in ancient times the character "八" (eight) was used to mean multiple or many, and it is said to have had the meaning of a sacred virgin who handled divine spirits. Also, in the case of groups of shrine maidens, the number "七" (seven) is used ( for example , the story of Takasano in the Kojiki ).
It is said to have originated from the shrine maidens who served food to the emperor and the gods during the [[Daijosai]] ceremony of the ancient [[Emperor Keikō]] . Later, these duties were carried out by Saijo, who were determined by divination at the Jingikan . Influenced by this, shrine maidens with similar roles were placed at other shrines , and they also became involved in Shinto rituals.
The shrine maiden dance and shrine maiden dance performed by the Yaotome are called "Yaoto Kagura" and "Yaotomai." In the past, the Yaotome of Kasuga Taisha and Itsukushima Shrine were well known, but today the Yaotome dances of Miho Shrine , Kotohiragu Shrine , and Toyokuni Shrine are also well known. It is also said that when shrine maiden dance itself was in danger due to government policy during the Meiji period , shrine maiden dance itself was transformed into a more artistic dance based on the Yaotome dance of Kasuga Taisha. [[ja:1979年2月3日]] [[ja:重要無形民俗文化財]] == See Also == * {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|en}}[[ja:伝統芸能|en]]

revision.text:
[[File:Sumiyoshi jinja Otaue.jpg|thumb|360px|Sumiyoshi Taisha Otaue Ritual.]] [[ja:八乙女]] [[ja:住吉踊り]] [[ja:ほら貝]] Yaotome refers to eight shrine maidens who mainly perform Kagura and dance (known as Miko Kagura and Miko Mai). They are also written as Yasha-me, Yajo, and Yaotome. They are also abbreviated to Yame. A sacred song that is said to have been composed during Empress Jingu 's '''visit''' to '''Tsukushi''' contains '''the lines ,''' "Who are the eight maidens? Yaotome so, the eight maidens of the heavenly God Ame Waka Miko."
== overview == The number of people was fixed at eight in later times, and in ancient times the character "八" (eight) was used to mean multiple or many, and it is said to have had the meaning of a sacred virgin who handled divine spirits. Also, in the case of groups of shrine maidens, the number "七" (seven) is used ( for example , the story of Takasano in the Kojiki ).
It is said to have originated from the shrine maidens who served food to the emperor and the gods during the [[Daijosai]] ceremony of the ancient [[Emperor Keikō]] . Later, these duties were carried out by Saijo, who were determined by divination at the Jingikan . Influenced by this, shrine maidens with similar roles were placed at other shrines , and they also became involved in Shinto rituals.
The shrine maiden dance and shrine maiden dance performed by the Yaotome are called "Yaoto Kagura" and "Yaotomai." In the past, the Yaotome of Kasuga Taisha and Itsukushima Shrine were well known, but today the Yaotome dances of Miho Shrine , Kotohiragu Shrine , and Toyokuni Shrine are also well known. It is also said that when shrine maiden dance itself was in danger due to government policy during the Meiji period , shrine maiden dance itself was transformed into a more artistic dance based on the Yaotome dance of Kasuga Taisha. [[ja:1979年2月3日]] [[ja:重要無形民俗文化財]] == See Also == * {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|en}}[[ja:伝統芸能|en]]
== External Links == * [https://www.sumiyoshitaisha.net/events/special.html#anchor01 Otaue (Official Sumiyoshi Taisha website)] Accessed September 22, 2009. * {{Cultural Heritage Online|302|103|住吉の御田植]]

revision.text:
[[File:Sumiyoshi jinja Otaue.jpg|thumb|360px|Sumiyoshi Taisha Otaue Ritual.]] [[ja:八乙女]] [[ja:住吉踊り]] [[ja:ほら貝]] Yaotome refers to eight shrine maidens who mainly perform Kagura and dance (known as Miko Kagura and Miko Mai). They are also written as Yasha-me, Yajo, and Yaotome. They are also abbreviated to Yame. A sacred song that is said to have been composed during Empress Jingu 's '''visit''' to '''Tsukushi''' contains '''the lines ,''' "Who are the eight maidens? Yaotome so, the eight maidens of the heavenly God Ame Waka Miko."
== overview == The number of people was fixed at eight in later times, and in ancient times the character "八" (eight) was used to mean multiple or many, and it is said to have had the meaning of a sacred virgin who handled divine spirits. Also, in the case of groups of shrine maidens, the number "七" (seven) is used ( for example , the story of Takasano in the Kojiki ).
It is said to have originated from the shrine maidens who served food to the emperor and the gods during the [[Daijosai]] ceremony of the ancient [[Emperor Keikō]] . Later, these duties were carried out by Saijo, who were determined by divination at the Jingikan . Influenced by this, shrine maidens with similar roles were placed at other shrines , and they also became involved in Shinto rituals.
The shrine maiden dance and shrine maiden dance performed by the Yaotome are called "Yaoto Kagura" and "Yaotomai." In the past, the Yaotome of Kasuga Taisha and Itsukushima Shrine were well known, but today the Yaotome dances of Miho Shrine , Kotohiragu Shrine , and Toyokuni Shrine are also well known. It is also said that when shrine maiden dance itself was in danger due to government policy during the Meiji period , shrine maiden dance itself was transformed into a more artistic dance based on the Yaotome dance of Kasuga Taisha. [[ja:1979年2月3日]] [[ja:重要無形民俗文化財]] == See Also == * {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|en}}[[ja:伝統芸能|en]]
== External Links == * [https://www.sumiyoshitaisha.net/events/special.html#anchor01 Otaue (Official Sumiyoshi Taisha website)] Accessed September 22, 2009. * {{Cultural Heritage Online|302|103|住吉の御田植]]

In the past, the Yaotome of Kasuga Taisha and Itsukushima Shrine were well known, but today the Yaotome dances of Miho Shrine , Kotohiragu Shrine , and Toyokuni Shrine are also well known. It is also said that when shrine maiden dance itself was in danger due to government policy during the Meiji period , shrine maiden dance itself was transformed into a more artistic dance based on the Yaotome dance of Kasuga Taisha. [[File:Sumiyoshi jinja Otaue.jpg|thumb|360px|Sumiyoshi Taisha Otaue Ritual.]] [[ja:八乙女]] [[ja:住吉踊り]] [[ja:ほら貝]] Yaotome refers to eight shrine maidens who mainly perform Kagura and dance (known as Miko Kagura and Miko Mai). They are also written as Yasha-me, Yajo, and Yaotome. They are also abbreviated to Yame. A sacred song that is said to have been composed during Empress Jingu 's '''visit''' to '''Tsukushi''' contains '''the lines ,''' "Who are the eight maidens? Yaotome so, the eight maidens of the heavenly God Ame Waka Miko."
== overview == The number of people was fixed at eight in later times, and in ancient times the character "八" (eight) was used to mean multiple or many, and it is said to have had the meaning of a sacred virgin who handled divine spirits. Also, in the case of groups of shrine maidens, the number "七" (seven) is used ( for example , the story of Takasano in the Kojiki ).
It is said to have originated from the shrine maidens who served food to the emperor and the gods during the [[Daijosai]] ceremony of the ancient [[Emperor Keikō]] . Later, these duties were carried out by Saijo, who were determined by divination at the Jingikan . Influenced by this, shrine maidens with similar roles were placed at other shrines , and they also became involved in Shinto rituals.
The shrine maiden dance and shrine maiden dance performed by the Yaotome are called "Yaoto Kagura" and "Yaotomai." In the past, the Yaotome of Kasuga Taisha and Itsukushima Shrine were well known, but today the Yaotome dances of Miho Shrine , Kotohiragu Shrine , and Toyokuni Shrine are also well known. It is also said that when shrine maiden dance itself was in danger due to government policy during the Meiji period , shrine maiden dance itself was transformed into a more artistic dance based on the Yaotome dance of Kasuga Taisha. revision.text:
[[File:Sumiyoshi jinja Otaue.jpg|thumb|360px|Sumiyoshi Taisha Otaue Ritual.]] [[ja:八乙女]] [[ja:住吉踊り]] [[ja:ほら貝]] Yaotome refers to eight shrine maidens who mainly perform Kagura and dance (known as Miko Kagura and Miko Mai). They are also written as Yasha-me, Yajo, and Yaotome. They are also abbreviated to Yame. A sacred song that is said to have been composed during Empress Jingu 's '''visit''' to '''Tsukushi''' contains '''the lines ,''' "Who are the eight maidens? Yaotome so, the eight maidens of the heavenly God Ame Waka Miko."
== overview == The number of people was fixed at eight in later times, and in ancient times the character "八" (eight) was used to mean multiple or many, and it is said to have had the meaning of a sacred virgin who handled divine spirits. Also, in the case of groups of shrine maidens, the number "七" (seven) is used ( for example , the story of Takasano in the Kojiki ).
It is said to have originated from the shrine maidens who served food to the emperor and the gods during the [[Daijosai]] ceremony of the ancient [[Emperor Keikō]] . Later, these duties were carried out by Saijo, who were determined by divination at the Jingikan . Influenced by this, shrine maidens with similar roles were placed at other shrines , and they also became involved in Shinto rituals.
The shrine maiden dance and shrine maiden dance performed by the Yaotome are called "Yaoto Kagura" and "Yaotomai." In the past, the Yaotome of Kasuga Taisha and Itsukushima Shrine were well known, but today the Yaotome dances of Miho Shrine , Kotohiragu Shrine , and Toyokuni Shrine are also well known. It is also said that when shrine maiden dance itself was in danger due to government policy during the Meiji period , shrine maiden dance itself was transformed into a more artistic dance based on the Yaotome dance of Kasuga Taisha. [[ja:1979年2月3日]] [[ja:重要無形民俗文化財]] == See Also == * {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|en}}[[ja:伝統芸能|en]]
== External Links == * [https://www.sumiyoshitaisha.net/events/special.html#anchor01 Otaue (Official Sumiyoshi Taisha website)] Accessed September 22, 2009. * {{Cultural Heritage Online|302|103|住吉の御田植]]

revision.text:
[[File:Sumiyoshi jinja Otaue.jpg|thumb|360px|Sumiyoshi Taisha Otaue Ritual.]] [[ja:八乙女]] [[ja:住吉踊り]] [[ja:ほら貝]] Yaotome refers to eight shrine maidens who mainly perform Kagura and dance (known as Miko Kagura and Miko Mai). They are also written as Yasha-me, Yajo, and Yaotome. They are also abbreviated to Yame. A sacred song that is said to have been composed during Empress Jingu 's '''visit''' to '''Tsukushi''' contains '''the lines ,''' "Who are the eight maidens? Yaotome so, the eight maidens of the heavenly God Ame Waka Miko."
== overview == The number of people was fixed at eight in later times, and in ancient times the character "八" (eight) was used to mean multiple or many, and it is said to have had the meaning of a sacred virgin who handled divine spirits. Also, in the case of groups of shrine maidens, the number "七" (seven) is used ( for example , the story of Takasano in the Kojiki ).
It is said to have originated from the shrine maidens who served food to the emperor and the gods during the [[Daijosai]] ceremony of the ancient [[Emperor Keikō]] . Later, these duties were carried out by Saijo, who were determined by divination at the Jingikan . Influenced by this, shrine maidens with similar roles were placed at other shrines , and they also became involved in Shinto rituals.
The shrine maiden dance and shrine maiden dance performed by the Yaotome are called "Yaoto Kagura" and "Yaotomai." In the past, the Yaotome of Kasuga Taisha and Itsukushima Shrine were well known, but today the Yaotome dances of Miho Shrine , Kotohiragu Shrine , and Toyokuni Shrine are also well known. It is also said that when shrine maiden dance itself was in danger due to government policy during the Meiji period , shrine maiden dance itself was transformed into a more artistic dance based on the Yaotome dance of Kasuga Taisha. [[ja:1979年2月3日]] [[ja:重要無形民俗文化財]] == See Also == * {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|en}}[[ja:伝統芸能|en]]
== External Links == * [https://www.sumiyoshitaisha.net/events/special.html#anchor01 Otaue (Official Sumiyoshi Taisha website)] Accessed September 22, 2009. * {{Cultural Heritage Online|302|103|住吉の御田植]]

[[File:Sumiyoshi jinja Otaue.jpg|thumb|360px|Sumiyoshi Taisha Otaue Ritual.]] [[ja:八乙女]] [[ja:住吉踊り]] [[ja:ほら貝]] Yaotome refers to eight shrine maidens who mainly perform Kagura and dance (known as Miko Kagura and Miko Mai). They are also written as Yasha-me, Yajo, and Yaotome. They are also abbreviated to Yame. A sacred song that is said to have been composed during Empress Jingu 's '''visit''' to '''Tsukushi''' contains '''the lines ,''' "Who are the eight maidens? Yaotome so, the eight maidens of the heavenly God Ame Waka Miko."
== overview == The number of people was fixed at eight in later times, and in ancient times the character "八" (eight) was used to mean multiple or many, and it is said to have had the meaning of a sacred virgin who handled divine spirits. Also, in the case of groups of shrine maidens, the number "七" (seven) is used ( for example , the story of Takasano in the Kojiki ).
It is said to have originated from the shrine maidens who served food to the emperor and the gods during the [[Daijosai]] ceremony of the ancient [[Emperor Keikō]] . Later, these duties were carried out by Saijo, who were determined by divination at the Jingikan . Influenced by this, shrine maidens with similar roles were placed at other shrines , and they also became involved in Shinto rituals.
The shrine maiden dance and shrine maiden dance performed by the Yaotome are called "Yaoto Kagura" and "Yaotomai." In the past, the Yaotome of Kasuga Taisha and Itsukushima Shrine were well known, but today the Yaotome dances of Miho Shrine , Kotohiragu Shrine , and Toyokuni Shrine are also well known. It is also said that when shrine maiden dance itself was in danger due to government policy during the Meiji period , shrine maiden dance itself was transformed into a more artistic dance based on the Yaotome dance of Kasuga Taisha. [[ja:1979年2月3日]] [[ja:重要無形民俗文化財]] == See Also == * {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|en}}[[ja:伝統芸能|en]]

[[File:Sumiyoshi jinja Otaue.jpg|thumb|360px|Sumiyoshi Taisha Otaue Ritual.]] [[ja:八乙女]] [[ja:住吉踊り]] [[ja:ほら貝]] Yaotome refers to eight shrine maidens who mainly perform Kagura and dance (known as Miko Kagura and Miko Mai). They are also written as Yasha-me, Yajo, and Yaotome. They are also abbreviated to Yame. A sacred song that is said to have been composed during Empress Jingu 's '''visit''' to '''Tsukushi''' contains '''the lines ,''' "Who are the eight maidens? Yaotome so, the eight maidens of the heavenly God Ame Waka Miko."
== overview == The number of people was fixed at eight in later times, and in ancient times the character "八" (eight) was used to mean multiple or many, and it is said to have had the meaning of a sacred virgin who handled divine spirits. Also, in the case of groups of shrine maidens, the number "七" (seven) is used ( for example , the story of Takasano in the Kojiki ).
It is said to have originated from the shrine maidens who served food to the emperor and the gods during the [[Daijosai]] ceremony of the ancient [[Emperor Keikō]] . Later, these duties were carried out by Saijo, who were determined by divination at the Jingikan . Influenced by this, shrine maidens with similar roles were placed at other shrines , and they also became involved in Shinto rituals.
The shrine maiden dance and shrine maiden dance performed by the Yaotome are called "Yaoto Kagura" and "Yaotomai." In the past, the Yaotome of Kasuga Taisha and Itsukushima Shrine were well known, but today the Yaotome dances of Miho Shrine , Kotohiragu Shrine , and Toyokuni Shrine are also well known. It is also said that when shrine maiden dance itself was in danger due to government policy during the Meiji period , shrine maiden dance itself was transformed into a more artistic dance based on the Yaotome dance of Kasuga Taisha. [[ja:1979年2月3日]] [[ja:重要無形民俗文化財]] == See Also == * {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|en}}[[ja:伝統芸能|en]]
