[[File:Sumiyoshi jinja Otaue.jpg|thumb|360px|Sumiyoshi Taisha Otaue Ritual.]] [[ja:八乙女]] [[ja:住吉踊り]] [[ja:ほら貝]] Yaotome refers to eight shrine maidens who mainly perform Kagura and dance (known as Miko Kagura and Miko Mai). They are also written as Yasha-me, Yajo, and Yaotome. They are also abbreviated to Yame. A sacred song that is said to have been composed during Empress Jingu 's '''visit''' to '''Tsukushi''' contains '''the lines ,''' "Who are the eight maidens? Yaotome so, the eight maidens of the heavenly God Ame Waka Miko."
== overview == The number of people was fixed at eight in later times, and in ancient times the character "八" (eight) was used to mean multiple or many, and it is said to have had the meaning of a sacred virgin who handled divine spirits. Also, in the case of groups of shrine maidens, the number "七" (seven) is used ( for example , the story of Takasano in the Kojiki ).
It is said to have originated from the shrine maidens who served food to the emperor and the gods during the [[Daijosai]] ceremony of the ancient [[Emperor Keikō]] . Later, these duties were carried out by Saijo, who were determined by divination at the Jingikan . Influenced by this, shrine maidens with similar roles were placed at other shrines , and they also became involved in Shinto rituals.
The shrine maiden dance and shrine maiden dance performed by the Yaotome are called "Yaoto Kagura" and "Yaotomai." In the past, the Yaotome of Kasuga Taisha and Itsukushima Shrine were well known, but today the Yaotome dances of Miho Shrine , Kotohiragu Shrine , and Toyokuni Shrine are also well known. It is also said that when shrine maiden dance itself was in danger due to government policy during the Meiji period , shrine maiden dance itself was transformed into a more artistic dance based on the Yaotome dance of Kasuga Taisha. [[ja:1979年2月3日]] [[ja:重要無形民俗文化財]] == See Also == * {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|en}}[[ja:伝統芸能|en]]

[[File:Sumiyoshi jinja Otaue.jpg|thumb|360px|Sumiyoshi Taisha Otaue Ritual.]] [[ja:八乙女]] [[ja:住吉踊り]] [[ja:ほら貝]] Yaotome refers to eight shrine maidens who mainly perform Kagura and dance (known as Miko Kagura and Miko Mai). They are also written as Yasha-me, Yajo, and Yaotome. They are also abbreviated to Yame. A sacred song that is said to have been composed during Empress Jingu 's '''visit''' to '''Tsukushi''' contains '''the lines ,''' "Who are the eight maidens? Yaotome so, the eight maidens of the heavenly God Ame Waka Miko."
== overview == The number of people was fixed at eight in later times, and in ancient times the character "八" (eight) was used to mean multiple or many, and it is said to have had the meaning of a sacred virgin who handled divine spirits. Also, in the case of groups of shrine maidens, the number "七" (seven) is used ( for example , the story of Takasano in the Kojiki ).
It is said to have originated from the shrine maidens who served food to the emperor and the gods during the [[Daijosai]] ceremony of the ancient [[Emperor Keikō]] . Later, these duties were carried out by Saijo, who were determined by divination at the Jingikan . Influenced by this, shrine maidens with similar roles were placed at other shrines , and they also became involved in Shinto rituals.
The shrine maiden dance and shrine maiden dance performed by the Yaotome are called "Yaoto Kagura" and "Yaotomai." In the past, the Yaotome of Kasuga Taisha and Itsukushima Shrine were well known, but today the Yaotome dances of Miho Shrine , Kotohiragu Shrine , and Toyokuni Shrine are also well known. It is also said that when shrine maiden dance itself was in danger due to government policy during the Meiji period , shrine maiden dance itself was transformed into a more artistic dance based on the Yaotome dance of Kasuga Taisha. [[File:Sumiyoshi jinja Otaue.jpg|thumb|360px|Sumiyoshi Taisha Otaue Ritual.]] [[ja:八乙女]] [[ja:住吉踊り]] [[ja:ほら貝]] Yaotome refers to eight shrine maidens who mainly perform Kagura and dance (known as Miko Kagura and Miko Mai). They are also written as Yasha-me, Yajo, and Yaotome. They are also abbreviated to Yame. A sacred song that is said to have been composed during Empress Jingu 's '''visit''' to '''Tsukushi''' contains '''the lines ,''' "Who are the eight maidens? Yaotome so, the eight maidens of the heavenly God Ame Waka Miko."
== overview == The number of people was fixed at eight in later times, and in ancient times the character "八" (eight) was used to mean multiple or many, and it is said to have had the meaning of a sacred virgin who handled divine spirits. Also, in the case of groups of shrine maidens, the number "七" (seven) is used ( for example , the story of Takasano in the Kojiki ).
It is said to have originated from the shrine maidens who served food to the emperor and the gods during the [[Daijosai]] ceremony of the ancient [[Emperor Keikō]] . Later, these duties were carried out by Saijo, who were determined by divination at the Jingikan . Influenced by this, shrine maidens with similar roles were placed at other shrines , and they also became involved in Shinto rituals.
The shrine maiden dance and shrine maiden dance performed by the Yaotome are called "Yaoto Kagura" and "Yaotomai." In the past, the Yaotome of Kasuga Taisha and Itsukushima Shrine were well known, but today the Yaotome dances of Miho Shrine , Kotohiragu Shrine , and Toyokuni Shrine are also well known. It is also said that when shrine maiden dance itself was in danger due to government policy during the Meiji period , shrine maiden dance itself was transformed into a more artistic dance based on the Yaotome dance of Kasuga Taisha. [[ja:1979年2月3日]] [[ja:重要無形民俗文化財]] == See Also == * {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|en}}[[ja:伝統芸能|en]]

[[File:Sumiyoshi jinja Otaue.jpg|thumb|360px|Sumiyoshi Taisha Otaue Ritual.]] [[ja:八乙女]] [[ja:住吉踊り]] [[ja:ほら貝]] Yaotome refers to eight shrine maidens who mainly perform Kagura and dance (known as Miko Kagura and Miko Mai). They are also written as Yasha-me, Yajo, and Yaotome. They are also abbreviated to Yame. A sacred song that is said to have been composed during Empress Jingu 's '''visit''' to '''Tsukushi''' contains '''the lines ,''' "Who are the eight maidens? Yaotome so, the eight maidens of the heavenly God Ame Waka Miko."
== overview == The number of people was fixed at eight in later times, and in ancient times the character "八" (eight) was used to mean multiple or many, and it is said to have had the meaning of a sacred virgin who handled divine spirits. Also, in the case of groups of shrine maidens, the number "七" (seven) is used ( for example , the story of Takasano in the Kojiki ).
It is said to have originated from the shrine maidens who served food to the emperor and the gods during the [[Daijosai]] ceremony of the ancient [[Emperor Keikō]] . Later, these duties were carried out by Saijo, who were determined by divination at the Jingikan . Influenced by this, shrine maidens with similar roles were placed at other shrines , and they also became involved in Shinto rituals.
The shrine maiden dance and shrine maiden dance performed by the Yaotome are called "Yaoto Kagura" and "Yaotomai." In the past, the Yaotome of Kasuga Taisha and Itsukushima Shrine were well known, but today the Yaotome dances of Miho Shrine , Kotohiragu Shrine , and Toyokuni Shrine are also well known. It is also said that when shrine maiden dance itself was in danger due to government policy during the Meiji period , shrine maiden dance itself was transformed into a more artistic dance based on the Yaotome dance of Kasuga Taisha. [[ja:1979年2月3日]] [[ja:重要無形民俗文化財]] == See Also == * {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|en}}[[ja:伝統芸能|en]]

[[File:Sumiyoshi jinja Otaue.jpg|thumb|360px|Sumiyoshi Taisha Otaue Ritual.]] [[ja:八乙女]] [[ja:住吉踊り]] [[ja:ほら貝]] Yaotome refers to eight shrine maidens who mainly perform Kagura and dance (known as Miko Kagura and Miko Mai). They are also written as Yasha-me, Yajo, and Yaotome. They are also abbreviated to Yame. A sacred song that is said to have been composed during Empress Jingu 's '''visit''' to '''Tsukushi''' contains '''the lines ,''' "Who are the eight maidens? Yaotome so, the eight maidens of the heavenly God Ame Waka Miko."
== overview == The number of people was fixed at eight in later times, and in ancient times the character "八" (eight) was used to mean multiple or many, and it is said to have had the meaning of a sacred virgin who handled divine spirits. Also, in the case of groups of shrine maidens, the number "七" (seven) is used ( for example , the story of Takasano in the Kojiki ).
It is said to have originated from the shrine maidens who served food to the emperor and the gods during the [[Daijosai]] ceremony of the ancient [[Emperor Keikō]] . Later, these duties were carried out by Saijo, who were determined by divination at the Jingikan . Influenced by this, shrine maidens with similar roles were placed at other shrines , and they also became involved in Shinto rituals.
The shrine maiden dance and shrine maiden dance performed by the Yaotome are called "Yaoto Kagura" and "Yaotomai." In the past, the Yaotome of Kasuga Taisha and Itsukushima Shrine were well known, but today the Yaotome dances of Miho Shrine , Kotohiragu Shrine , and Toyokuni Shrine are also well known. It is also said that when shrine maiden dance itself was in danger due to government policy during the Meiji period , shrine maiden dance itself was transformed into a more artistic dance based on the Yaotome dance of Kasuga Taisha. [[ja:1979年2月3日]] [[ja:重要無形民俗文化財]] == See Also == * {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|en}}[[ja:伝統芸能|en]]

[[File:Sumiyoshi jinja Otaue.jpg|thumb|360px|Sumiyoshi Taisha Otaue Ritual.]] [[ja:八乙女]] [[ja:住吉踊り]] [[ja:ほら貝]] Yaotome refers to eight shrine maidens who mainly perform Kagura and dance (known as Miko Kagura and Miko Mai). They are also written as Yasha-me, Yajo, and Yaotome. They are also abbreviated to Yame. A sacred song that is said to have been composed during Empress Jingu 's '''visit''' to '''Tsukushi''' contains '''the lines ,''' "Who are the eight maidens? Yaotome so, the eight maidens of the heavenly God Ame Waka Miko."
== overview == The number of people was fixed at eight in later times, and in ancient times the character "八" (eight) was used to mean multiple or many, and it is said to have had the meaning of a sacred virgin who handled divine spirits. Also, in the case of groups of shrine maidens, the number "七" (seven) is used ( for example , the story of Takasano in the Kojiki ).
It is said to have originated from the shrine maidens who served food to the emperor and the gods during the [[Daijosai]] ceremony of the ancient [[Emperor Keikō]] . Later, these duties were carried out by Saijo, who were determined by divination at the Jingikan . Influenced by this, shrine maidens with similar roles were placed at other shrines , and they also became involved in Shinto rituals.
The shrine maiden dance and shrine maiden dance performed by the Yaotome are called "Yaoto Kagura" and "Yaotomai." In the past, the Yaotome of Kasuga Taisha and Itsukushima Shrine were well known, but today the Yaotome dances of Miho Shrine , Kotohiragu Shrine , and Toyokuni Shrine are also well known. It is also said that when shrine maiden dance itself was in danger due to government policy during the Meiji period , shrine maiden dance itself was transformed into a more artistic dance based on the Yaotome dance of Kasuga Taisha. [[ja:1979年2月3日]] [[ja:重要無形民俗文化財]] == See Also == * {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|en}}[[ja:伝統芸能|en]]

[[File:Sumiyoshi jinja Otaue.jpg|thumb|360px|Sumiyoshi Taisha Otaue Ritual.]] [[ja:八乙女]] [[ja:住吉踊り]] [[ja:ほら貝]] Yaotome refers to eight shrine maidens who mainly perform Kagura and dance (known as Miko Kagura and Miko Mai). They are also written as Yasha-me, Yajo, and Yaotome. They are also abbreviated to Yame. A sacred song that is said to have been composed during Empress Jingu 's '''visit''' to '''Tsukushi''' contains '''the lines ,''' "Who are the eight maidens? Yaotome so, the eight maidens of the heavenly God Ame Waka Miko."
== overview == The number of people was fixed at eight in later times, and in ancient times the character "八" (eight) was used to mean multiple or many, and it is said to have had the meaning of a sacred virgin who handled divine spirits. Also, in the case of groups of shrine maidens, the number "七" (seven) is used ( for example , the story of Takasano in the Kojiki ).
It is said to have originated from the shrine maidens who served food to the emperor and the gods during the [[Daijosai]] ceremony of the ancient [[Emperor Keikō]] . Later, these duties were carried out by Saijo, who were determined by divination at the Jingikan . Influenced by this, shrine maidens with similar roles were placed at other shrines , and they also became involved in Shinto rituals.
The shrine maiden dance and shrine maiden dance performed by the Yaotome are called "Yaoto Kagura" and "Yaotomai." In the past, the Yaotome of Kasuga Taisha and Itsukushima Shrine were well known, but today the Yaotome dances of Miho Shrine , Kotohiragu Shrine , and Toyokuni Shrine are also well known. It is also said that when shrine maiden dance itself was in danger due to government policy during the Meiji period , shrine maiden dance itself was transformed into a more artistic dance based on the Yaotome dance of Kasuga Taisha. [[ja:1979年2月3日]] [[ja:重要無形民俗文化財]] == See Also == * {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|en}}[[ja:伝統芸能|en]]

[[File:Sumiyoshi jinja Otaue.jpg|thumb|360px|Sumiyoshi Taisha Otaue Ritual.]] [[ja:八乙女]] [[ja:住吉踊り]] [[ja:ほら貝]] Yaotome refers to eight shrine maidens who mainly perform Kagura and dance (known as Miko Kagura and Miko Mai). They are also written as Yasha-me, Yajo, and Yaotome. They are also abbreviated to Yame. A sacred song that is said to have been composed during Empress Jingu 's '''visit''' to '''Tsukushi''' contains '''the lines ,''' "Who are the eight maidens? Yaotome so, the eight maidens of the heavenly God Ame Waka Miko."
== overview == The number of people was fixed at eight in later times, and in ancient times the character "八" (eight) was used to mean multiple or many, and it is said to have had the meaning of a sacred virgin who handled divine spirits. Also, in the case of groups of shrine maidens, the number "七" (seven) is used ( for example , the story of Takasano in the Kojiki ).
It is said to have originated from the shrine maidens who served food to the emperor and the gods during the [[Daijosai]] ceremony of the ancient [[Emperor Keikō]] . Later, these duties were carried out by Saijo, who were determined by divination at the Jingikan . Influenced by this, shrine maidens with similar roles were placed at other shrines , and they also became involved in Shinto rituals.
The shrine maiden dance and shrine maiden dance performed by the Yaotome are called "Yaoto Kagura" and "Yaotomai." In the past, the Yaotome of Kasuga Taisha and Itsukushima Shrine were well known, but today the Yaotome dances of Miho Shrine , Kotohiragu Shrine , and Toyokuni Shrine are also well known. It is also said that when shrine maiden dance itself was in danger due to government policy during the Meiji period , shrine maiden dance itself was transformed into a more artistic dance based on the Yaotome dance of Kasuga Taisha. [[ja:1979年2月3日]] [[ja:重要無形民俗文化財]] == See Also == * {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|en}}[[ja:伝統芸能|en]]

[[File:Sumiyoshi jinja Otaue.jpg|thumb|360px|Sumiyoshi Taisha Otaue Ritual.]] [[ja:八乙女]] [[ja:住吉踊り]] [[ja:ほら貝]] Yaotome refers to eight shrine maidens who mainly perform Kagura and dance (known as Miko Kagura and Miko Mai). They are also written as Yasha-me, Yajo, and Yaotome. They are also abbreviated to Yame. A sacred song that is said to have been composed during Empress Jingu 's '''visit''' to '''Tsukushi''' contains '''the lines ,''' "Who are the eight maidens? Yaotome so, the eight maidens of the heavenly God Ame Waka Miko."
== overview == The number of people was fixed at eight in later times, and in ancient times the character "八" (eight) was used to mean multiple or many, and it is said to have had the meaning of a sacred virgin who handled divine spirits. Also, in the case of groups of shrine maidens, the number "七" (seven) is used ( for example , the story of Takasano in the Kojiki ).
It is said to have originated from the shrine maidens who served food to the emperor and the gods during the [[Daijosai]] ceremony of the ancient [[Emperor Keikō]] . Later, these duties were carried out by Saijo, who were determined by divination at the Jingikan . Influenced by this, shrine maidens with similar roles were placed at other shrines , and they also became involved in Shinto rituals.
The shrine maiden dance and shrine maiden dance performed by the Yaotome are called "Yaoto Kagura" and "Yaotomai." In the past, the Yaotome of Kasuga Taisha and Itsukushima Shrine were well known, but today the Yaotome dances of Miho Shrine , Kotohiragu Shrine , and Toyokuni Shrine are also well known. It is also said that when shrine maiden dance itself was in danger due to government policy during the Meiji period , shrine maiden dance itself was transformed into a more artistic dance based on the Yaotome dance of Kasuga Taisha. [[ja:1979年2月3日]] [[ja:重要無形民俗文化財]] == See Also == * {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|en}}[[ja:伝統芸能|en]]
== External Links == * [https://www.sumiyoshitaisha.net/events/special.html#anchor01 Otaue (Official Sumiyoshi Taisha website)] Accessed September 22, 2009. * {{Cultural Heritage Online|302|103|住吉の御田植]]

[[File:Sumiyoshi jinja Otaue.jpg|thumb|360px|Sumiyoshi Taisha Otaue Ritual.]] [[ja:八乙女]] [[ja:住吉踊り]] [[ja:ほら貝]] Yaotome refers to eight shrine maidens who mainly perform Kagura and dance (known as Miko Kagura and Miko Mai). They are also written as Yasha-me, Yajo, and Yaotome. They are also abbreviated to Yame. A sacred song that is said to have been composed during Empress Jingu 's '''visit''' to '''Tsukushi''' contains '''the lines ,''' "Who are the eight maidens? Yaotome so, the eight maidens of the heavenly God Ame Waka Miko."
== overview == The number of people was fixed at eight in later times, and in ancient times the character "八" (eight) was used to mean multiple or many, and it is said to have had the meaning of a sacred virgin who handled divine spirits. Also, in the case of groups of shrine maidens, the number "七" (seven) is used ( for example , the story of Takasano in the Kojiki ).
It is said to have originated from the shrine maidens who served food to the emperor and the gods during the [[Daijosai]] ceremony of the ancient [[Emperor Keikō]] . Later, these duties were carried out by Saijo, who were determined by divination at the Jingikan . Influenced by this, shrine maidens with similar roles were placed at other shrines , and they also became involved in Shinto rituals.
The shrine maiden dance and shrine maiden dance performed by the Yaotome are called "Yaoto Kagura" and "Yaotomai." In the past, the Yaotome of Kasuga Taisha and Itsukushima Shrine were well known, but today the Yaotome dances of Miho Shrine , Kotohiragu Shrine , and Toyokuni Shrine are also well known. It is also said that when shrine maiden dance itself was in danger due to government policy during the Meiji period , shrine maiden dance itself was transformed into a more artistic dance based on the Yaotome dance of Kasuga Taisha. [[File:Sumiyoshi jinja Otaue.jpg|thumb|360px|Sumiyoshi Taisha Otaue Ritual.]] [[ja:八乙女]] [[ja:住吉踊り]] [[ja:ほら貝]] Yaotome refers to eight shrine maidens who mainly perform Kagura and dance (known as Miko Kagura and Miko Mai). They are also written as Yasha-me, Yajo, and Yaotome. They are also abbreviated to Yame. A sacred song that is said to have been composed during Empress Jingu 's '''visit''' to '''Tsukushi''' contains '''the lines ,''' "Who are the eight maidens? Yaotome so, the eight maidens of the heavenly God Ame Waka Miko."
== overview == The number of people was fixed at eight in later times, and in ancient times the character "八" (eight) was used to mean multiple or many, and it is said to have had the meaning of a sacred virgin who handled divine spirits. Also, in the case of groups of shrine maidens, the number "七" (seven) is used ( for example , the story of Takasano in the Kojiki ).
It is said to have originated from the shrine maidens who served food to the emperor and the gods during the [[Daijosai]] ceremony of the ancient [[Emperor Keikō]] . Later, these duties were carried out by Saijo, who were determined by divination at the Jingikan . Influenced by this, shrine maidens with similar roles were placed at other shrines , and they also became involved in Shinto rituals.
The shrine maiden dance and shrine maiden dance performed by the Yaotome are called "Yaoto Kagura" and "Yaotomai." In the past, the Yaotome of Kasuga Taisha and Itsukushima Shrine were well known, but today the Yaotome dances of Miho Shrine , Kotohiragu Shrine , and Toyokuni Shrine are also well known. It is also said that when shrine maiden dance itself was in danger due to government policy during the Meiji period , shrine maiden dance itself was transformed into a more artistic dance based on the Yaotome dance of Kasuga Taisha. [[File:Sumiyoshi jinja Otaue.jpg|thumb|360px|Sumiyoshi Taisha Otaue Ritual.]] [[ja:八乙女]] [[ja:住吉踊り]] [[ja:ほら貝]] Yaotome refers to eight shrine maidens who mainly perform Kagura and dance (known as Miko Kagura and Miko Mai). They are also written as Yasha-me, Yajo, and Yaotome. They are also abbreviated to Yame. A sacred song that is said to have been composed during Empress Jingu 's '''visit''' to '''Tsukushi''' contains '''the lines ,''' "Who are the eight maidens? Yaotome so, the eight maidens of the heavenly God Ame Waka Miko."
== overview == The number of people was fixed at eight in later times, and in ancient times the character "八" (eight) was used to mean multiple or many, and it is said to have had the meaning of a sacred virgin who handled divine spirits. Also, in the case of groups of shrine maidens, the number "七" (seven) is used ( for example , the story of Takasano in the Kojiki ).
It is said to have originated from the shrine maidens who served food to the emperor and the gods during the [[Daijosai]] ceremony of the ancient [[Emperor Keikō]] . Later, these duties were carried out by Saijo, who were determined by divination at the Jingikan . Influenced by this, shrine maidens with similar roles were placed at other shrines , and they also became involved in Shinto rituals.
The shrine maiden dance and shrine maiden dance performed by the Yaotome are called "Yaoto Kagura" and "Yaotomai." In the past, the Yaotome of Kasuga Taisha and Itsukushima Shrine were well known, but today the Yaotome dances of Miho Shrine , Kotohiragu Shrine , and Toyokuni Shrine are also well known. It is also said that when shrine maiden dance itself was in danger due to government policy during the Meiji period , shrine maiden dance itself was transformed into a more artistic dance based on the Yaotome dance of Kasuga Taisha. [[ja:1979th year 2月3日]] [[ja:重要無形民俗文化財]] == See Also == * {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|en}}[[ja:伝統芸能|en]]

[[File:Sumiyoshi jinja Otaue.jpg|thumb|360px|Sumiyoshi Taisha Otaue Ritual.]] [[ja:八乙女]] [[ja:住吉踊り]] [[ja:ほら貝]] Yaotome refers to eight shrine maidens who mainly perform Kagura and dance (known as Miko Kagura and Miko Mai). They are also written as Yasha-me, Yajo, and Yaotome. They are also abbreviated to Yame. A sacred song that is said to have been composed during Empress Jingu 's '''visit''' to '''Tsukushi''' contains '''the lines ,''' "Who are the eight maidens? Yaotome so, the eight maidens of the heavenly God Ame Waka Miko."
== overview == The number of people was fixed at eight in later times, and in ancient times the character "八" (eight) was used to mean multiple or many, and it is said to have had the meaning of a sacred virgin who handled divine spirits. Also, in the case of groups of shrine maidens, the number "七" (seven) is used ( for example , the story of Takasano in the Kojiki ).
It is said to have originated from the shrine maidens who served food to the emperor and the gods during the [[Daijosai]] ceremony of the ancient [[Emperor Keikō]] . Later, these duties were carried out by Saijo, who were determined by divination at the Jingikan . Influenced by this, shrine maidens with similar roles were placed at other shrines , and they also became involved in Shinto rituals.
The shrine maiden dance and shrine maiden dance performed by the Yaotome are called "Yaoto Kagura" and "Yaotomai." In the past, the Yaotome of Kasuga Taisha and Itsukushima Shrine were well known, but today the Yaotome dances of Miho Shrine , Kotohiragu Shrine , and Toyokuni Shrine are also well known. It is also said that when shrine maiden dance itself was in danger due to government policy during the Meiji period , shrine maiden dance itself was transformed into a more artistic dance based on the Yaotome dance of Kasuga Taisha. [[ja:1979th year 2月3日]] [[ja:重要無形民俗文化財]] == See Also == * {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|en}}[[ja:伝統芸能|en]]

[[File:Sumiyoshi jinja Otaue.jpg|thumb|360px|Sumiyoshi Taisha Otaue Ritual.]] [[ja:八乙女]] [[ja:住吉踊り]] [[ja:ほら貝]] Yaotome refers to eight shrine maidens who mainly perform Kagura and dance (known as Miko Kagura and Miko Mai). They are also written as Yasha-me, Yajo, and Yaotome. They are also abbreviated to Yame. A sacred song that is said to have been composed during Empress Jingu 's '''visit''' to '''Tsukushi''' contains '''the lines ,''' "Who are the eight maidens? Yaotome so, the eight maidens of the heavenly God Ame Waka Miko."
== overview == The number of people was fixed at eight in later times, and in ancient times the character "八" (eight) was used to mean multiple or many, and it is said to have had the meaning of a sacred virgin who handled divine spirits. Also, in the case of groups of shrine maidens, the number "七" (seven) is used ( for example , the story of Takasano in the Kojiki ).
It is said to have originated from the shrine maidens who served food to the emperor and the gods during the [[Daijosai]] ceremony of the ancient [[Emperor Keikō]] . Later, these duties were carried out by Saijo, who were determined by divination at the Jingikan . Influenced by this, shrine maidens with similar roles were placed at other shrines , and they also became involved in Shinto rituals.
The shrine maiden dance and shrine maiden dance performed by the Yaotome are called "Yaoto Kagura" and "Yaotomai." In the past, the Yaotome of Kasuga Taisha and Itsukushima Shrine were well known, but today the Yaotome dances of Miho Shrine , Kotohiragu Shrine , and Toyokuni Shrine are also well known. It is also said that when shrine maiden dance itself was in danger due to government policy during the Meiji period , shrine maiden dance itself was transformed into a more artistic dance based on the Yaotome dance of Kasuga Taisha. [[ja:1979th year 2月3日]] [[ja:重要無形民俗文化財]] == See Also == * {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|en}}[[ja:伝統芸能|en]]

revision.text:
[[File:Sumiyoshi jinja Otaue.jpg|thumb|360px|Sumiyoshi Taisha Otaue Ritual.]]
Yaotome refers to eight shrine maidens who mainly perform Kagura and dance (known as Miko Kagura and Miko Mai). They are also written as Yasha-me, Yajo, and Yaotome. They are also abbreviated to Yame. A sacred song that is said to have been composed during Empress Jingu 's '''visit''' to '''Tsukushi''' contains '''the lines ,''' "Who are the eight maidens? Yaotome so, the eight maidens of the heavenly God Ame Waka Miko."
== overview == The number of people was fixed at eight in later times, and in ancient times the character "八" (eight) was used to mean multiple or many, and it is said to have had the meaning of a sacred virgin who handled divine spirits. Also, in the case of groups of shrine maidens, the number "七" (seven) is used ( for example , the story of Takasano in the Kojiki ).
It is said to have originated from the shrine maidens who served food to the emperor and the gods during the [[Daijosai]] ceremony of the ancient [[Emperor Keikō]] . Later, these duties were carried out by Saijo, who were determined by divination at the Jingikan . Influenced by this, shrine maidens with similar roles were placed at other shrines , and they also became involved in Shinto rituals.
The shrine maiden dance and shrine maiden dance performed by the Yaotome are called "Yaoto Kagura" and "Yaotomai." In the past, the Yaotome of Kasuga Taisha and Itsukushima Shrine were well known, but today the Yaotome dances of Miho Shrine , Kotohiragu Shrine , and Toyokuni Shrine are also well known. It is also said that when shrine maiden dance itself was in danger due to government policy during the Meiji period , shrine maiden dance itself was transformed into a more artistic dance based on the Yaotome dance of Kasuga Taisha.
== See Also == * {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|en}}
== External Links == * [https://www.sumiyoshitaisha.net/events/special.html#anchor01 Otaue (Official Sumiyoshi Taisha website)] Accessed September 22, 2009. * {{Cultural Heritage Online|302|103|住吉の御田植]]

32 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:八乙女 up to rev 101931810
revision.text:
'''八乙女'''（やおとめ）とは、主に[[神楽]]や[[舞]]（いわゆる[[巫女神楽]]・[[巫女舞]]）をもって奉仕する8人の[[巫女]]のこと。'''八社女'''・'''八少女'''・'''八乎止女'''とも書く。また、略して'''八女'''（やめ）とも。 神功皇后の筑紫行幸啓の折に詠まれたと伝わる神歌に、「八女（やおとめ）は 誰（た）か 八女（やおとめ）そ 天（あめ）に坐す 天若御子（あめわかみこ）の 神の八女（やおとめ）」とある。 ==概要== 人数が8人と定まったのは後世の事であり、古くは「八」の字は複数あるいは多くという意味で使われていたもので、神霊を扱う神聖な処女の意味があったと言われている。また、巫女の群遊の場合には「七」という数字が用いられる事例がある（『[[古事記]]』[[高佐士野]]の説話など）。
[[古代]]の[[景行天皇]]の[[大嘗祭]]の際に[[天皇]]と[[神々]]に食事を奉仕した巫女に由来するとされている。後には[[神祇官]]において卜定められた[[采女]]がこうした任務にあたった。この影響を受けて他の[[神社]]においても同様の役目の巫女が置かれ、更には神事にも関わるようになったとされている。
八乙女による巫女神楽や巫女舞を「八乙女神楽」・「八乙女舞」と呼ぶ。古くは[[春日大社]]や[[厳島神社]]の八乙女がよく知られていたが、今日では[[美保神社]]や[[金刀比羅宮]]、[[豊国神社 (京都市)|豊国神社]]の八乙女舞も有名になっている。また、[[明治時代]]に政府の方針で巫女舞そのものが危機に瀕した際には、春日大社の八乙女舞を元にして巫女舞そのものがより芸術性の高い舞へと変革されていったともいわれている。
==地名== *八乙女浦（[[山形県]][[鶴岡市]]由良）：[[蜂子皇子]]を、舞を踊って出迎えた八人の美しい乙女に由来する。 *八乙女洞窟（山形県鶴岡市由良）：明治時代の酒田地震により、崩れた岩で入口が塞がっている。 *八乙女公園（[[秋田県]][[大仙市]]長野字八乙女）：同地にあった八乙女城に由来する。 *[[八乙女 (仙台市)|八乙女]]（[[宮城県]][[仙台市]][[泉区 (仙台市)|泉区]]） *[[八乙女 (いすみ市)|八乙女]]（[[千葉県]][[いすみ市]]） *八乙女山（[[富山県]][[南砺市]]）：平成の大合併で新市名を八乙女市にする案もあったが、市議会の多数決により南砺市に決定した。また京都の[[五山送り火|大文字送り火]]に似た八文字焼きという行事が毎年行われている。
==関連項目== *[[巫女]]
{{shinto-stub}} {{デフォルトソート:やおとめ}} [[Category:巫女]] [[Category:神楽]] [[Category:日本の名数8|おとめ]]

==地名== *八乙女浦（[[山形県]][[鶴岡市]]由良）：[[蜂子皇子]]を、舞を踊って出迎えた八人の美しい乙女に由来する。 *八乙女洞窟（山形県鶴岡市由良）：明治時代の酒田地震により、崩れた岩で入口が塞がっている。 *八乙女公園（[[秋田県]][[大仙市]]長野字八乙女）：同地にあった八乙女城に由来する。 *[[八乙女 (仙台市)|八乙女]]（[[宮城県]][[仙台市]][[泉区 (仙台市)|泉区]]） *[[八乙女 (いすみ市)|八乙女]]（[[千葉県]][[いすみ市]]） *八乙女山（[[富山県]][[南砺市]]）：平成の大合併で新市名を八乙女市にする案もあったが、市議会の多数決により南砺市に決定した。また京都の[[五山送り火|大文字送り火]]に似た八文字焼きという行事が毎年行われている。

[[File:Sumiyoshi jinja Otaue.jpg|thumb|360px|Sumiyoshi Taisha Otaue Ritual.]] Yaotome refers to eight shrine maidens who mainly perform Kagura and dance (known as Miko Kagura and Miko Mai). They are also written as Yasha-me, Yajo, and Yaotome. They are also abbreviated to Yame. A sacred song that is said to have been composed during Empress Jingu 's '''visit''' to '''Tsukushi''' contains '''the lines ,''' "Who are the eight maidens? Yaotome so, the eight maidens of the heavenly God Ame Waka Miko."
== overview == The number of people was fixed at eight in later times, and in ancient times the character "八" (eight) was used to mean multiple or many, and it is said to have had the meaning of a sacred virgin who handled divine spirits. Also, in the case of groups of shrine maidens, the number "七" (seven) is used ( for example , the story of Takasano in the Kojiki ).
It is said to have originated from the shrine maidens who served food to the emperor and the gods during the [[Daijosai]] ceremony of the ancient [[Emperor Keikō]] . Later, these duties were carried out by Saijo, who were determined by divination at the Jingikan . Influenced by this, shrine maidens with similar roles were placed at other shrines , and they also became involved in Shinto rituals.
The shrine maiden dance and shrine maiden dance performed by the Yaotome are called "Yaoto Kagura" and "Yaotomai." In the past, the Yaotome of Kasuga Taisha and Itsukushima Shrine were well known, but today the Yaotome dances of Miho Shrine , Kotohiragu Shrine , and Toyokuni Shrine are also well known. It is also said that when shrine maiden dance itself was in danger due to government policy during the Meiji period , shrine maiden dance itself was transformed into a more artistic dance based on the Yaotome dance of Kasuga Taisha.
== See Also == * {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}}
== External Links == * [https://www.sumiyoshitaisha.net/events/special.html#anchor01 Otaue (Official Sumiyoshi Taisha website)] Accessed September 22, 2009. * {{Cultural Heritage Online|302|103|住吉の御田植]]

revision.comment: Bot: remove deleted category Festivals in Osaka
revision.text:
[[File:Sumiyoshi jinja Otaue.jpg|thumb|360px|Sumiyoshi Taisha Otaue Ritual.]] Yaotome refers to eight shrine maidens who mainly perform Kagura and dance (known as Miko Kagura and Miko Mai). They are also written as Yasha-me, Yajo, and Yaotome. They are also abbreviated to Yame. A sacred song that is said to have been composed during Empress Jingu 's '''visit''' to '''Tsukushi''' contains '''the lines ,''' "Who are the eight maidens? Yaotome so, the eight maidens of the heavenly God Ame Waka Miko."
== overview == The number of people was fixed at eight in later times, and in ancient times the character "八" (eight) was used to mean multiple or many, and it is said to have had the meaning of a sacred virgin who handled divine spirits. Also, in the case of groups of shrine maidens, the number "七" (seven) is used ( for example , the story of Takasano in the Kojiki ).
It is said to have originated from the shrine maidens who served food to the emperor and the gods during the [[Daijosai]] ceremony of the ancient [[Emperor Keikō]] . Later, these duties were carried out by Saijo, who were determined by divination at the Jingikan . Influenced by this, shrine maidens with similar roles were placed at other shrines , and they also became involved in Shinto rituals.
The shrine maiden dance and shrine maiden dance performed by the Yaotome are called "Yaoto Kagura" and "Yaotomai." In the past, the Yaotome of Kasuga Taisha and Itsukushima Shrine were well known, but today the Yaotome dances of Miho Shrine , Kotohiragu Shrine , and Toyokuni Shrine are also well known. It is also said that when shrine maiden dance itself was in danger due to government policy during the Meiji period , shrine maiden dance itself was transformed into a more artistic dance based on the Yaotome dance of Kasuga Taisha.
== See Also == * {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Theatre of Japan|qq=}}
== External Links == * [https://www.sumiyoshitaisha.net/events/special.html#anchor01 Otaue (Official Sumiyoshi Taisha website)] Accessed September 22, 2009. * {{Cultural Heritage Online|302|103|住吉の御田植]]

revision.comment: Text replacement - "Famous Numbers of" to "Enumerations of"
revision.text:
[[File:Sumiyoshi jinja Otaue.jpg|thumb|360px|Sumiyoshi Taisha Otaue Ritual.]] Yaotome refers to eight shrine maidens who mainly perform Kagura and dance (known as Miko Kagura and Miko Mai). They are also written as Yasha-me, Yajo, and Yaotome. They are also abbreviated to Yame. A sacred song that is said to have been composed during Empress Jingu 's '''visit''' to '''Tsukushi''' contains '''the lines ,''' "Who are the eight maidens? Yaotome so, the eight maidens of the heavenly God Ame Waka Miko."
== overview == The number of people was fixed at eight in later times, and in ancient times the character "八" (eight) was used to mean multiple or many, and it is said to have had the meaning of a sacred virgin who handled divine spirits. Also, in the case of groups of shrine maidens, the number "七" (seven) is used ( for example , the story of Takasano in the Kojiki ).
It is said to have originated from the shrine maidens who served food to the emperor and the gods during the [[Daijosai]] ceremony of the ancient [[:en:Emperor Keikō|Emperor Keikō]] . Later, these duties were carried out by Saijo, who were determined by divination at the Jingikan . Influenced by this, shrine maidens with similar roles were placed at other shrines , and they also became involved in Shinto rituals.
The shrine maiden dance and shrine maiden dance performed by the Yaotome are called "Yaoto Kagura" and "Yaotomai." In the past, the Yaotome of Kasuga Taisha and Itsukushima Shrine were well known, but today the Yaotome dances of Miho Shrine , Kotohiragu Shrine , and Toyokuni Shrine are also well known. It is also said that when shrine maiden dance itself was in danger due to government policy during the Meiji period , shrine maiden dance itself was transformed into a more artistic dance based on the Yaotome dance of Kasuga Taisha.
== See Also == * {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Theatre of Japan|qq=}}
== External Links == * [https://www.sumiyoshitaisha.net/events/special.html#anchor01 Otaue (Official Sumiyoshi Taisha website)] Accessed September 22, 2009. * {{Cultural Heritage Online|302|103|住吉の御田植]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:八乙女]][[ko:야오토메]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11258587]] Wikidata name: yaotome [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:28, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Sumiyoshi jinja Otaue.jpg|thumb|360px|Sumiyoshi Taisha Otaue Ritual.]] Yaotome refers to eight shrine maidens who mainly perform Kagura and dance (known as Miko Kagura and Miko Mai). They are also written as Yasha-me, Yajo, and Yaotome. They are also abbreviated to Yame. A sacred song that is said to have been composed during Empress Jingu 's '''visit''' to '''Tsukushi''' contains '''the lines ,''' "Who are the eight maidens? Yaotome so, the eight maidens of the heavenly God Ame Waka Miko."
== overview == The number of people was fixed at eight in later times, and in ancient times the character "八" (eight) was used to mean multiple or many, and it is said to have had the meaning of a sacred virgin who handled divine spirits. Also, in the case of groups of shrine maidens, the number "七" (seven) is used ( for example , the story of Takasano in the Kojiki ).
It is said to have originated from the shrine maidens who served food to the emperor and the gods during the [[Daijosai]] ceremony of the ancient [[:en:Emperor Keikō|Emperor Keikō]] . Later, these duties were carried out by Saijo, who were determined by divination at the Jingikan . Influenced by this, shrine maidens with similar roles were placed at other shrines , and they also became involved in Shinto rituals.
The shrine maiden dance and shrine maiden dance performed by the Yaotome are called "Yaoto Kagura" and "Yaotomai." In the past, the Yaotome of Kasuga Taisha and Itsukushima Shrine were well known, but today the Yaotome dances of Miho Shrine , Kotohiragu Shrine , and Toyokuni Shrine are also well known. It is also said that when shrine maiden dance itself was in danger due to government policy during the Meiji period , shrine maiden dance itself was transformed into a more artistic dance based on the Yaotome dance of Kasuga Taisha.
== See Also == * {{ill|Theatre of Japan|lt=Traditional performing arts of Japan|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Theatre of Japan|qq=}}
== External Links == * [https://www.sumiyoshitaisha.net/events/special.html#anchor01 Otaue (Official Sumiyoshi Taisha website)] Accessed September 22, 2009. * {{Cultural Heritage Online|302|103|住吉の御田植]]
{{Coord|34|36|40.86|N|135|29|32.83|E|region:JP-27_type:event_scale:10000|display=title]]

It is said to have originated from the shrine maidens who served food to the emperor and the gods during the [[Daijosai]] ceremony of the ancient [[:en:Emperor Keikō|Emperor Keikō]] . Later, these duties were carried out by Saijo, who were determined by divination at the Jingikan . Influenced by this, shrine maidens with similar roles were placed at other shrines , and they also became involved in Shinto rituals.
The shrine maiden dance and shrine maiden dance performed by the Yaotome are called "Yaoto Kagura" and "Yaotomai." In the past, the Yaotome of Kasuga Taisha and Itsukushima Shrine were well known, but today the Yaotome dances of Miho Shrine , Kotohiragu Shrine , and Toyokuni Shrine are also well known. It is also said that when shrine maiden dance itself was in danger due to government policy during the Meiji period , shrine maiden dance itself was transformed into a more artistic dance based on the Yaotome dance of Kasuga Taisha.

They are also written as Yasha-me, Yajo, and Yaotome. They are also abbreviated to Yame. == overview == The number of people was fixed at eight in later times, and in ancient times the character "八" (eight) was used to mean multiple or many, and it is said to have had the meaning of a sacred virgin who handled divine spirits. Also, in the case of groups of shrine maidens, the number "七" (seven) is used ( for example , the story of Takasano in the Kojiki ).
It is said to have originated from the shrine maidens who served food to the emperor and the gods during the [[Daijosai]] ceremony of the ancient [[:en:Emperor Keikō|Emperor Keikō]] . Later, these duties were carried out by Saijo, who were determined by divination at the Jingikan . Influenced by this, shrine maidens with similar roles were placed at other shrines , and they also became involved in Shinto rituals.
The shrine maiden dance and shrine maiden dance performed by the Yaotome are called "Yaoto Kagura" and "Yaotomai." In the past, the Yaotome of Kasuga Taisha and Itsukushima Shrine were well known, but today the Yaotome dances of Miho Shrine , Kotohiragu Shrine , and Toyokuni Shrine are also well known. It is also said that when shrine maiden dance itself was in danger due to government policy during the Meiji period , shrine maiden dance itself was transformed into a more artistic dance based on the Yaotome dance of Kasuga Taisha.
